stage-0.heading: Why the names blur ŌĆö and why that's good news
stage-0.body:
You've been asked to stand up "Claude" for your team, and the vocabulary already fights you. There's a **Desktop app** with a tab called *Code*, a **terminal command** called `claude`, a cloud thing called **Cowork**, and a separate vendor's tool called **Codex**. People in your channel use these words interchangeably and inconsistently, and the docs don't always disambiguate them either.

Here is the load-bearing fact that makes the rest of this course tractable:

> **Claude Code's terminal CLI and the Desktop app's *Code* tab are the same engine reading the same config** ŌĆö the same `CLAUDE.md`, MCP servers, hooks, skills, and settings. (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart)

The official quickstart puts it plainly: *"Desktop runs the same engine as the CLI with a graphical interface. You can run both simultaneously on the same project, and they share configuration (CLAUDE.md files, MCP servers, hooks, skills, and settings)."*

That is the whole insight of stage 0. You are not configuring four products. You are configuring **one product** ŌĆö Claude Code ŌĆö and the front-ends are interchangeable windows onto it. When your teammate on macOS uses the GUI and your teammate on Linux uses the terminal, they drive the same engine off the same files in the repo. That is what makes a *team* setup possible: you write the config once, commit it, and every face of the tool obeys it.

The rest of this stage names each face precisely, shows you the config files they all share (with real, copy-able formats and exact paths), and draws the one genuine boundary that *isn't* just a UI difference: Codex, a different vendor's agent that does not read Claude's config and is a deliberate complement, not a substitute.

stage-0.heading: What "Claude Code" actually is
stage-0.body:
**Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding tool**: a long-running agent that reads your repository, plans, edits files, runs commands, and iterates ŌĆö driven either from a terminal or a GUI. "Agentic" is the operative word. It is not a chat box that emits code for you to paste. It holds a session, takes tools (read file, write file, run bash, call an MCP server), and works in a loop until the task is done or it needs you.

### One engine, multiple front-ends

Mentally, separate two layers:

- **The engine** ŌĆö the agent loop, the tool-use machinery, the model calls, and the *config resolver* that loads your instructions, permissions, MCP servers, hooks, and skills at session start.
- **The front-end** ŌĆö the surface you sit in front of: a terminal TUI, a Desktop GUI pane, an IDE side panel, or a web/cloud session.

The engine is the same across front-ends. The front-end only changes ergonomics. The terminal gives you keyboard-driven speed and easy scripting; the Desktop GUI adds a sidebar of parallel sessions (each in its own git worktree), drag-and-drop panes (chat, diff, terminal, file editor, preview), visual diff review with line-level comments, live app preview, and GitHub PR monitoring with auto-merge. Neither changes *what the agent can do* or *which config it reads*.

### Why this matters for a team lead

If the engine and config are shared, then your job ŌĆö "set up Claude for the team" ŌĆö collapses to **authoring shared config files and committing them**. You do not onboard people per-surface. A `CLAUDE.md` you commit today is read identically whether a teammate runs `claude` in a tmux pane or clicks the Code tab in the Desktop app. Get the files right once and the surface choice becomes a personal-preference detail, not an infrastructure decision.

One practical note from the quickstart: the Desktop app *bundles* Claude Code ŌĆö you don't need to install Node.js or the CLI separately to use the Code tab. If you also want the `claude` terminal command, you install the CLI separately. Same engine either way.

stage-0.heading: The five faces, mapped
stage-0.body:
Here are the names you'll hear, what each actually *is*, and whether it shares Claude Code's config.

| Name | What it is | Engine | Reads shared Claude config? | Runs where |
|------|-----------|--------|------------------------------|-----------|
| **Claude Code CLI** (`claude`) | Terminal TUI; the canonical surface | Claude Code | Yes | Local (your machine) |
| **Desktop ŌåÆ Code tab** | GUI front-end on the *same* engine | Claude Code | Yes | Local, Remote (Anthropic cloud), or SSH |
| **Desktop ŌåÆ Cowork tab** | Autonomous background agent in a cloud VM | Claude Code (remote) | Yes (same infra as Claude Code on the web) | Cloud VM |
| **Desktop ŌåÆ Chat tab** | claude.ai-style chat, **no file access** | Not the coding agent | No | n/a |
| **OpenAI Codex** | A *different vendor's* agentic coding tool | Codex (OpenAI) | **No** ŌĆö reads `AGENTS.md` + `~/.codex/config.toml` | Local CLI, IDE ext, Codex Cloud |

The Desktop app, per the quickstart, has exactly three tabs: *"**Chat**: General conversation with no file access, similar to claude.ai. **Cowork**: An autonomous background agent that works on tasks in a cloud VM with its own environment. **Code**: An interactive coding assistant with direct access to your local files."* (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart)

### Reading the table

The first three rows are *the same tool*. Within the Code tab itself you pick where it runs ŌĆö **Local** (your files directly), **Remote** (Anthropic's cloud infrastructure, sessions continue even if you close the app, same infra as Claude Code on the web), or **SSH** (your own server / VM / dev container; Desktop installs Claude Code on the remote machine automatically the first time). The **Chat** tab is the odd one out within Desktop ŌĆö it is the claude.ai chat experience bolted into the same window, with no repo access; it is not the coding agent and won't read your `CLAUDE.md`. **Codex** is the genuine other product, covered at the end of this stage and throughout the course as a deliberate second agent ŌĆö not a synonym for Claude Code.

stage-0.heading: The shared config: what makes them one tool
stage-0.body:
**Shared config** is the set of files Claude Code reads at session start ŌĆö instructions, settings, MCP servers, hooks, and skills ŌĆö that *every* Claude Code front-end resolves the same way. This is the literal mechanism behind "one tool, many front-ends." When the docs say the CLI and the Code tab "share configuration," they mean these files on disk.

There are five categories. You'll meet each in depth later in the course; here is the map so the words stop blurring.

| Category | What it controls | Canonical location(s) |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Instructions** | Persistent natural-language guidance to the agent | `./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md` (project), `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (user), `.claude/rules/*.md` (path-scoped) |
| **Settings** | Permissions, env vars, model, behavior toggles | `.claude/settings.json`, `.claude/settings.local.json`, `~/.claude/settings.json`, `managed-settings.json` (policy) |
| **MCP servers** | External tools/data the agent can call | `.mcp.json` (project root), `~/.claude.json` (user/local) |
| **Hooks** | Shell commands that fire at lifecycle events (deterministic enforcement) | `.claude/settings.json` or a plugin's `hooks/hooks.json` |
| **Skills** | On-demand procedures invokable as `/name` | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, `~/.claude/skills/...`, plugins |

### The recurring two-axis pattern

Nearly every config type follows the same **scope ├Ś shared-ness** layout, which is worth internalizing now because it repeats:

- **Project scope** lives in `.claude/` *in the repo* and is committed ŌĆö this is your **team** config.
- **User scope** lives in `~/.claude/` ŌĆö personal, applies across all your projects.
- **Local scope** is `*.local.*` in the repo, gitignored ŌĆö personal overrides for *this* project. (MCP is the one exception: its "local" scope lives in `~/.claude.json`, not in the repo ŌĆö covered in the MCP section.)
- **Managed/policy** scope is IT-deployed and cannot be overridden by individuals.

Get this layout in your head and every config file in the course slots into a known grid: *who does it apply to* (this project / all my projects / everyone in the org) and *is it committed* (team-shared / personal).

stage-0.heading: Instructions: CLAUDE.md (the team's shared brain)
stage-0.body:
`CLAUDE.md` is the file you'll author first and touch most. It's plain markdown, loaded into context in full at the start of *every* session, on *every* front-end. Treat it as the thing you'd otherwise re-explain to a new teammate: build commands, conventions, architecture, "always do X" rules.

### Locations and load order

The docs list them broadest-to-most-specific; later (more specific) files appear later in context. (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory)

| Scope | Location | Shared with |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Managed policy | `/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md` (Linux/WSL), `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md` (macOS), `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md` (Windows) | All users in org (cannot be excluded) |
| User | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Just you, all projects |
| Project | `./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md` | **Team, via source control** |
| Local | `./CLAUDE.local.md` (gitignore it) | Just you, this project |

Claude walks *up* the directory tree from your cwd and concatenates every `CLAUDE.md` (and `CLAUDE.local.md`) it finds, ordered filesystem-root-down so instructions closest to where you launched are read last. Nested `CLAUDE.md` files in subdirectories load *on demand* when Claude reads files there ŌĆö useful in monorepos.

### Generating and importing

Run `/init` to have Claude analyze the repo and draft a starting `CLAUDE.md` (if one exists, it suggests improvements rather than overwriting). You can also pull in other files with `@`-imports ŌĆö resolved relative to the importing file, recursive to a max depth of four hops, and still loaded in full into context at launch:

```markdown
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code
Use plan mode for changes under `src/billing/`.
```

That `@AGENTS.md` import is the canonical bridge to Codex: the docs state *"Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md`"*, so importing it lets both agents share one instruction source. (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory)

### Gotchas

- **It's context, not enforcement.** The docs are explicit: CLAUDE.md is *"delivered as a user message after the system prompt"* and *"there's no guarantee of strict compliance."* To *block* an action regardless of what the model decides, use a **PreToolUse hook** or `permissions.deny`, not a CLAUDE.md sentence.
- **Keep it under ~200 lines.** The docs recommend targeting under 200 lines; longer files consume more context and *reduce* adherence. Move procedures to skills and path-specific guidance to `.claude/rules/` (which can be scoped to globs via `paths:` frontmatter so they only load for matching files).
- **Conflicts are silent.** If two CLAUDE.md files contradict, *"Claude may pick one arbitrarily."* Audit periodically; in monorepos use the `claudeMdExcludes` setting to skip other teams' files.
- **Don't confuse it with auto memory.** Claude also keeps a separate machine-local auto-memory at `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md` that *Claude* writes; `CLAUDE.md` is the file *you* write and commit. Only `CLAUDE.md` is team-shared via git.

stage-0.heading: Settings & permissions: settings.json
stage-0.body:
`settings.json` is the JSON config for behavior ŌĆö most importantly **permissions**, which decide what the agent may do without asking. Same scope grid as CLAUDE.md, with a precise precedence.

### Locations and precedence (highest wins)

```
1. Managed policy   (managed-settings.json ŌĆö cannot be overridden)
2. Command-line args
3. .claude/settings.local.json   (local ŌĆö personal, gitignored)
4. .claude/settings.json         (project ŌĆö committed, TEAM config)
5. ~/.claude/settings.json        (user ŌĆö all projects)
```

The managed policy file is literally named `managed-settings.json` and lives in the OS policy directory: `/etc/claude-code/` (Linux/WSL), `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/` (macOS), `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\` (Windows). (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)

### Example

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json",
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(npm run lint)",
      "Bash(npm run test:*)",
      "Read(~/.zshrc)"
    ],
    "ask": [
      "Bash(git push:*)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",
      "Read(./.env.*)",
      "Read(./secrets/**)"
    ]
  },
  "env": { "CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TELEMETRY": "1" },
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
}
```

### Permission rule syntax

Rules are `Tool(pattern)`. `Bash(npm run test:*)` matches a command prefix; `Read`/`Write` take glob paths (`*` = one segment, `**` = recursive). The three lists are `allow` (run silently), `ask` (prompt), `deny` (block). **Permission rules behave differently from other settings: they *merge* across scopes rather than override, and deny wins on conflict.** So a managed `deny` can't be unlocked by a project `allow`. (For technical enforcement the docs steer you to managed settings ŌĆö `permissions.deny`, `sandbox.enabled`, `env` ŌĆö and reserve CLAUDE.md for behavioral guidance.)

### Why this is the heart of a team setup

Permissions are the difference between "the agent is a productivity tool" and "the agent `rm -rf`'d something at 2am." Because the project `settings.json` is committed, you author the team's safety envelope *once* and every front-end ŌĆö terminal, Code tab, Cowork ŌĆö enforces it identically. Personal experiments go in the gitignored `settings.local.json` so nobody has to touch the shared file to try something. (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)

stage-0.heading: External tools: .mcp.json
On-demand procedures: skills (SKILL.md)
stage-0.body:
A **skill** is a packaged procedure the agent loads *only when used*, invokable as `/name`. Skills are how you keep CLAUDE.md small: *"a skill's body loads only when it's used, so long reference material costs almost nothing until you need it."* They follow the open **Agent Skills** standard (agentskills.io), which other AI tools also read; Claude Code extends it with invocation control, subagent execution, and dynamic context injection.

### Format

A skill is a directory whose entrypoint is `SKILL.md` ŌĆö YAML frontmatter plus markdown instructions. Only `description` is strictly needed (the *directory name* becomes the command); set `name` to override the invocation name:

```yaml
---
name: deploy
description: Deploy the application to production. Use when the user asks to ship or release.
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Deploy the application:
1. Run the test suite
2. Build the application
3. Push the release tag
```

The `description` is what lets Claude decide to auto-load the skill; `disable-model-invocation: true` makes it `/`-only (no autonomous triggering). You can also inject live context with a `` !`command` `` line, whose output is inlined before Claude sees the skill. A skill folder can carry supporting files Claude reads on demand:

```text
my-skill/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ SKILL.md          # required entrypoint
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ reference.md      # loaded only when needed
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ scripts/validate.sh
```

### Locations (same scope grid)

| Location | Path | Applies to |
|----------|------|-----------|
| Enterprise | via managed settings | All users in your org |
| Personal | `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | All your projects |
| Project | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | **This project (team-shared)** |
| Plugin | `<plugin>/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Where the plugin is enabled |

Name collisions resolve **enterprise > personal > project**, and any of these overrides a bundled skill of the same name (e.g. a project `code-review` skill replaces the built-in `/code-review`). Plugin skills use a `plugin-name:skill-name` namespace so they can't conflict. Note that **custom commands have merged into skills**: a file at `.claude/commands/deploy.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` both create `/deploy`; if both exist, the skill wins. (Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)

stage-0.heading: Bundling it all: plugins and marketplaces
stage-0.body:
Once your team has several skills, hooks, and MCP servers, you don't want each teammate to copy files by hand. A **plugin** is a self-contained directory bundling those components (skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, LSP servers, monitors); a **marketplace** is a git-hosted catalog of plugins your team installs from. This is the distribution mechanism for shared config.

### Plugin manifest

```json
// .claude-plugin/plugin.json
{
  "name": "quality-review-plugin",
  "description": "Adds a quality-review skill for quick code reviews",
  "version": "1.0.0"
}
```

If you include a manifest, **`name` is the only required field** (`description` and `version` are optional but recommended). Per the docs, *"the `.claude-plugin/` directory contains the `plugin.json` file. All other directories (`commands/`, `agents/`, `skills/`, `output-styles/`, `themes/`, `monitors/`, `hooks/`) must be at the plugin root, not inside `.claude-plugin/`."*

### Marketplace catalog

```json
// .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
{
  "name": "my-plugins",
  "owner": { "name": "Your Name" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "quality-review-plugin",
      "source": "./plugins/quality-review-plugin",
      "description": "Adds a quality-review skill for quick code reviews"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Install flow

```shell
/plugin marketplace add ./my-marketplace      # or a GitHub repo
/plugin install quality-review-plugin@my-plugins
```

Push the marketplace repo, teammates run those two commands, and everyone has the same skills/hooks/MCP servers. **Versioning gotcha (verbatim from the reference):** *"Setting `version` pins the plugin to that version string, so users only receive updates when you bump it. If omitted, Claude Code falls back to the git commit SHA, so every commit is treated as a new version."* So: set `version` and you must bump it on every release or teammates keep the cached copy; omit `version` and each commit auto-counts as new (good for internal, fast-moving plugins). Marketplace plugins are copied into a local cache (`~/.claude/plugins/cache`), and the old version is cleaned up ~7 days after an update. (Sources: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces, https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference)

stage-0.heading: The faces in practice: when to use what
stage-0.body:
All Claude Code front-ends share config, so the choice is about *ergonomics and where the work runs*, not capability.

| You want to... | Use | Why |
|----------------|-----|-----|
| Script the agent, pipe output, run in CI/tmux, work over SSH on a server | **CLI** (`claude`) | Keyboard-driven, composable, the canonical surface; the only option on Linux (no Desktop on Linux) |
| Run several tasks in parallel, review diffs visually, preview a running app | **Desktop ŌåÆ Code** | GUI adds parallel sessions (each in its own git worktree), line-level diff review, live preview, PR auto-merge |
| Kick off long autonomous work that survives closing your laptop | **Code tab ŌåÆ Remote**, **Cowork**, or CLI/Desktop "send to cloud" | Runs in a cloud VM on the same infra as Claude Code on the web |
| Just chat, no repo access | **Desktop ŌåÆ Chat** | claude.ai in the same window; *not* the coding agent |
| A second, cheaper agent for high-volume keystroke-level iteration | **Codex** (next section) | Different vendor, different cost/limit profile |

### Practical guidance for a team

Don't standardize the *surface*; standardize the *config*. Let people use the terminal or the GUI by taste ŌĆö they're driving the same engine off the same committed `.claude/` directory. What you enforce as a lead is the contents of `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/settings.json` (especially permissions), `.mcp.json`, and your plugin marketplace. And note the hard constraint: per the quickstart, *"the desktop app is not available on Linux; use the CLI instead"* ŌĆö so any Linux teammates are on the CLI regardless. That's one more reason the *config*, not the surface, is the unit of standardization.

stage-0.heading: The one real boundary: Codex is a different tool
stage-0.body:
Everything above is one product. **OpenAI Codex is not.** It's a separate vendor's agentic coding tool with its *own* engine, config, and surfaces (CLI TUI, IDE extension, and Codex Cloud). Critically for this stage's thesis: **Codex does not read Claude Code's config.** It reads its own.

### Codex's config files

```toml
# ~/.codex/config.toml   (project override: .codex/config.toml, loaded when you trust the project)
model = "gpt-5.5"
approval_policy = "on-request"   # untrusted | on-request | never
sandbox_mode = "workspace-write" # read-only | workspace-write | danger-full-access
model_reasoning_effort = "high"
```

Those `approval_policy` and `sandbox_mode` value sets are the documented valid options; `model` is illustrative (use whatever model id your account exposes). (Source: https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic)

### The shared-instruction bridge

The two ecosystems meet at **`AGENTS.md`**, the cross-tool open standard (agents.md). Codex reads `AGENTS.md` natively for instructions; it discovers them from your Codex home (`~/.codex/AGENTS.md`) down through the git root to your cwd, and per its docs *"concatenates files from the root down... files closer to your current directory override earlier guidance because they appear later in the combined prompt"* (capped at `project_doc_max_bytes`, 32 KiB default). Keep your durable instructions in `AGENTS.md` and have `CLAUDE.md` import it:

```markdown
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code-specific
Use plan mode for changes under `src/billing/`.
```

A symlink (`ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md`) also works when you have nothing Claude-specific to add (on Windows the import is preferred, since symlinks need admin/Developer Mode). Now one instruction source feeds both agents. Permissions, MCP, hooks, and skills still differ ŌĆö only the prose instructions are shared.

### Two distinct config worlds

| | Claude Code | Codex |
|--|-------------|-------|
| Instructions | `CLAUDE.md` (imports/symlinks `AGENTS.md`) | `AGENTS.md` (read directly) |
| Settings/permissions | `.claude/settings.json` | `~/.codex/config.toml` |
| Approval model | `permissions` allow/ask/deny | `approval_policy` + `sandbox_mode` |

(Sources: https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic, https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md)

stage-0.heading: Both, not either: the Claude Code + Codex pattern
stage-0.body:
Among practitioners, **Claude Code vs Codex is contested ŌĆö and the productive resolution is to run both**, routing work by their different strengths rather than picking a winner. (Source: https://dev.to/_46ea277e677b888e0cd13/claude-code-vs-codex-2026-what-500-reddit-developers-really-think-31pb)

The two positions in that survey of ~500 developers:

- **Claude-quality:** Claude Code is rated higher quality (~67% blind-comparison win rate), leads SWE-bench, and has full MCP support ŌĆö but ships with tighter usage limits.
- **Codex-efficiency:** Codex is cheaper (~4x fewer tokens), leads Terminal-Bench, and the community shorthand is *"Codex for keystroke, Claude Code for commits."*

### What "Codex for keystroke, Claude Code for commits" means operationally

Use the cheaper, token-efficient agent (Codex) for high-volume, low-stakes iteration ŌĆö the keystroke-level churn where you'd burn through Claude's tighter limits fast. Bring in the higher-quality agent (Claude Code) for the consequential, commit-worthy work: the refactor, the tricky bug, the change you'll actually ship. This is a *routing* decision, and because both read a shared `AGENTS.md`, you can move a task between them without rewriting your project's ground rules.

### What this means for your team setup

Design for two agents from the start. Put durable project knowledge in `AGENTS.md` so both read it; keep Claude-specific enforcement (permissions, hooks, skills) in `.claude/`; and treat the two agents' differing cost/limit profiles as a *budget tool*, not a loyalty test. This is contested terrain ŌĆö present it to your team as a tradeoff table, not a mandate. (These quality/cost figures are community-survey claims, not vendor benchmarks; treat them as directional, and re-check against your own usage.)

stage-0.heading: Common pitfalls and the mental model to keep
stage-0.body:
### Pitfalls

- **Treating Desktop's tabs as one thing.** *Code* is the agent; *Chat* has no file access and ignores your repo config; *Cowork* runs remotely. Conflating them leads to "why didn't it see my CLAUDE.md?" ŌĆö because you were in the Chat tab.
- **Expecting CLAUDE.md to *enforce* anything.** It's delivered as a user message ŌĆö context, not a guardrail, with no compliance guarantee. Anything that must *always* happen (block a path, run a lint before commit) belongs in a **PreToolUse hook** or `permissions.deny`, which the client enforces regardless of model behavior.
- **Committing secrets in `.mcp.json` or settings.** These files are team-shared. Use `${ENV_VAR}` (or `${VAR:-default}`) interpolation; keep real secrets in the environment.
- **Putting personal tweaks in the committed file.** Use `settings.local.json` and `CLAUDE.local.md` (gitignored) so experiments don't churn the team's shared config or leak your sandbox URLs.
- **Confusing MCP's "local" scope with `settings.local.json`.** MCP local-scoped servers live in `~/.claude.json` (home), not in the repo. Only *project*-scoped MCP (`.mcp.json`) is committed and team-shared.
- **Assuming Codex reads `CLAUDE.md`.** It does not ŌĆö it reads `AGENTS.md`. Bridge via an `@AGENTS.md` import or symlink, or maintain two instruction sources knowingly.
- **Plugin updates that don't update.** A pinned `version` in `plugin.json` must be bumped every release; otherwise teammates keep the cached copy. Omit it and each commit SHA counts as a new version.

### The mental model to carry into the rest of the course

Hold two sentences:

1. *Claude Code is one engine; the CLI, the Code tab, and Cowork are interchangeable front-ends reading the same `.claude/` config ŌĆö so I configure the **files**, not the surface.*
2. *Codex is a separate agent with its own config; the only shared seam is `AGENTS.md`, and the smart pattern is to run both and route by strength.*

Every later stage ŌĆö permissions, hooks, skills, MCP, plugins, multi-agent orchestration ŌĆö is just depth on one of the config categories you met here. The blur is gone: there is one tool with many faces, one genuine sibling tool, and a small grid of files that ties the whole thing to your repo.

stage-1.heading: The question behind the question
stage-1.body:
When you stand up shared agentic-coding infrastructure, the first thing your team will ask is deceptively simple: *"Which one do I open?"* They mean: terminal, desktop app, the thing my non-engineer PM is using, the OpenAI tool half the team prefers. The wrong answer wastes weeks. A backend dev who only knows the GUI will hand-edit JSON that should have been a `claude -p` one-liner in CI. An ops teammate who never hears about Cowork will keep doing by hand the cross-app drudgery that surface was built to absorb. A reviewer who assumes Codex and Claude Code share config will wonder why their carefully written `CLAUDE.md` is invisible to half the team.

The honest answer is **not one tool ŌĆö it's a menu**, and most items on the menu are the *same capability projected onto a different surface*, with one critical exception (Codex, which is a different vendor entirely). This stage maps the menu precisely: what each surface is, what it can and cannot do, where its config lives, and the failure modes you hit when you pick wrong.

### The mental model: one engine, several surfaces, one foreign agent

Three of the five things on this menu ŌĆö the terminal CLI, the Desktop **Code** tab, and headless mode ŌĆö are the *same Claude Code engine*. The official desktop docs say it plainly: "Desktop runs the same engine as the CLI with a graphical interface... they share configuration (CLAUDE.md files, MCP servers, hooks, skills, and settings)" ([desktop quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart)). Cowork is a fourth surface of Anthropic's agentic capability aimed at non-developers. Codex is a *separate product from OpenAI* with its own engine and its own config tree. Hold that shape in your head: **don't reason about "Claude Code the GUI vs Claude Code the CLI" as two products** ŌĆö they are one product reading one config, shown two ways.

stage-1.heading: Surface 1 ŌĆö the CLI (the terminal): the scriptable core
stage-1.body:
The CLI is `claude` in your terminal. It is the canonical, lowest-level surface: everything the other Claude Code surfaces do, this can do, plus the things *only* a terminal can do (pipe stdin, redirect stdout, exit codes, run inside CI with no display).

### What it is

An interactive REPL when you run `claude` bare, and a one-shot tool when you add `-p`. It reads the full configuration stack at startup: `CLAUDE.md` files up the directory tree, `~/.claude/` user config, MCP servers from `.mcp.json`, hooks, skills, and `settings.json`.

### Why a team standardizes on it as the base layer

- **Scriptable and automatable.** It composes with the rest of your shell. A `package.json` script can call it; a Makefile target can call it; a git hook can call it.
- **Reproducible.** With the right flags (see headless, below) you get identical behavior across machines.
- **Universal.** It is the only surface available on Linux ŌĆö the docs state "The desktop app is not available on Linux; use the CLI instead" ([desktop quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart)). Your CI runners and remote boxes are almost always Linux, so the CLI is the only surface that runs *everywhere your code runs*.

### Concrete: starting an interactive session

```bash
cd ~/projects/godel
claude                          # interactive REPL in this repo
claude --model claude-opus-4-8  # pin a model
claude --add-dir ../shared-lib  # grant access to a sibling dir
```

Inside the REPL, `/memory` lists which `CLAUDE.md`, `CLAUDE.local.md`, and rules files loaded (and toggles auto memory), `/config` changes settings, and `/skill-name` invokes a skill. The point of the CLI is that *all* of these are also reachable non-interactively ŌĆö which is the next surface.

### One menu, one table

| Surface | Engine | OS | Headless? | Config read |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CLI (`claude`) | Claude Code | macOS / Windows / **Linux** | yes (`-p`) | full Claude Code stack |
| Headless (`claude -p`) | Claude Code | same as CLI | yes (it *is* the mode) | full stack, or none with `--bare` |
| Desktop **Code** tab | Claude Code | macOS / Windows only | no (interactive) | same stack as CLI |
| Cowork | Anthropic agent (cloud VM) | macOS / Windows desktop | n/a (autonomous) | connectors, not CLAUDE.md |
| Codex | **OpenAI** | macOS / Windows / Linux | yes (`codex exec`) | `~/.codex` (TOML) |

stage-1.heading: Surface 2 ŌĆö headless mode: the same engine, no human in the loop
stage-1.body:
Headless mode is not a separate program. It is the CLI run **non-interactively** with `-p` (alias `--print`): one prompt in, output out, then exit. This is what you put in scripts, git hooks, and CI. Per the docs, this non-interactive mode is a **terminal-CLI capability ŌĆö the Desktop GUI is interactive only** ([headless docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless)).

### The shape of a headless call

```bash
claude -p "What does the auth module do?"
```

It reads stdin, so it composes like any Unix filter:

```bash
cat build-error.txt | claude -p 'concisely explain the root cause of this build error' > output.txt
```

As of Claude Code v2.1.128, piped stdin is capped at 10MB; beyond that, Claude Code exits with a clear error and a non-zero status, so write to a file and reference the path in the prompt instead.

### Structured output for scripts

`--output-format` controls the response shape ŌĆö `text` (default), `json` (result + session id + cost metadata), or `stream-json` (newline-delimited events for live streaming). For schema-constrained output, combine `--output-format json` with `--json-schema`; the structured payload lands in the `structured_output` field:

```bash
claude -p "Extract the main function names from auth.py" \
  --output-format json \
  --json-schema '{"type":"object","properties":{"functions":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"}}},"required":["functions"]}' \
  | jq '.structured_output'
```

The `json` payload includes `total_cost_usd` and a per-model cost breakdown, so a scripted caller can track spend per invocation without opening a dashboard. For live streaming, pair `--output-format stream-json` with `--verbose --include-partial-messages` and filter `text_delta` events with `jq`.

### Auto-approving tools (the part everyone gets wrong first)

In an interactive session you approve each tool use. In headless mode there is no human to approve, so an un-pre-approved tool **aborts the run**. You grant permission up front:

```bash
claude -p "Run the test suite and fix any failures" \
  --allowedTools "Bash,Read,Edit"
```

`--allowedTools` uses [permission-rule syntax](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings#permission-rule-syntax), so you can scope Bash narrowly. The trailing space-then-`*` is load-bearing:

```bash
claude -p "Look at my staged changes and create an appropriate commit" \
  --allowedTools "Bash(git diff *),Bash(git log *),Bash(git status *),Bash(git commit *)"
```

The docs are explicit: `Bash(git diff *)` allows any command starting with `git diff`, and "the space before `*` is important: without it, `Bash(git diff*)` would also match `git diff-index`." For a whole-session baseline instead of listing tools, pass a [permission mode](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes): `--permission-mode acceptEdits` (auto-writes files and auto-approves common fs commands like `mkdir`, `touch`, `mv`, `cp` ŌĆö other shell/network calls still need an `--allowedTools` entry) or `--permission-mode dontAsk` (denies anything not in `permissions.allow` or the read-only command set ŌĆö the locked-down CI choice).

### `--bare`: reproducibility for CI

`claude --bare -p ...` skips auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and `CLAUDE.md`. The docs call this "the recommended mode for scripted and SDK calls" and say it "will become the default for `-p` in a future release." The motivation is sharp: *a hook in a teammate's `~/.claude` or an MCP server in the repo's `.mcp.json` won't silently change your CI result, because bare mode never reads them.* In bare mode Claude still has Bash, file-read, and file-edit tools; you pass everything else explicitly:

```bash
claude --bare -p "Summarize this file" --allowedTools "Read" \
  --settings ./ci-settings.json \
  --mcp-config ./ci-mcp.json
```

Note bare mode skips OAuth/keychain reads ŌĆö Anthropic auth must come from `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or an `apiKeyHelper` in the JSON passed to `--settings` (Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry use their usual provider credentials).

### Use it as a project linter / reviewer

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint:claude": "git diff main | claude -p \"you are a typo linter. for each typo in this diff, report filename:line on one line and the issue on the next. return nothing else.\""
  }
}
```

Piping the diff means Claude doesn't even need Bash permission to read it. Add `--bare` for CI so the runner's ambient config can't change the result.

stage-1.heading: Surface 3 ŌĆö the Desktop Code tab: the same engine with a GUI
stage-1.body:
The Desktop app's **Code** tab is Claude Code with a graphical interface. The single most important fact to drill into your team, straight from the docs: "Desktop runs the same engine as the CLI with a graphical interface. You can run both simultaneously on the same project, and they share configuration (CLAUDE.md files, MCP servers, hooks, skills, and settings)" ([desktop quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart)). They do not have divergent behavior. Note the app is macOS/Windows only (universal macOS build; Windows x64 and ARM64) ŌĆö there is no Linux desktop, and Claude Code requires a Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise subscription.

### What the GUI adds over the terminal

The Desktop app is described as "built for running multiple sessions side by side" with a visual review loop:

- **Visual diff review.** Claude proposes a change; you see a file-by-file diff with Accept/Reject buttons and can comment on specific lines, which Claude reads and revises. Clicking **Review code** has Claude evaluate its own diffs and leave inline suggestions.
- **Drag-and-drop panes.** Chat, diff, integrated terminal (open with **Ctrl+`**), file editor, and live app **Preview** (run your dev server inside the app; Claude can view the running app, test endpoints, and inspect logs).
- **Parallel sessions, each in its own git worktree.** This is the headline capability: open multiple sessions from the sidebar, "each in its own Git worktree," so concurrent tasks don't stomp on each other's working tree. A tasks pane shows the subagents and background commands a session is running; a *side chat* lets you ask a question without derailing the main thread.
- **PR monitoring + auto-merge, scheduled tasks, remote/SSH environments.** It monitors CI check results and "can automatically fix failures or merge the PR once all checks pass"; it runs scheduled tasks (daily review, weekly dep audit, briefings); and sessions run **Local**, **Remote** (Anthropic cloud ŌĆö "continue even if you close the app"), or over **SSH** (it installs Claude Code on the remote machine the first time you connect).

### The permission modes mirror the CLI

| Desktop mode | CLI equivalent | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Ask permissions (default) | (interactive default) | Approve before every edit |
| Auto accept edits | `--permission-mode acceptEdits` | Auto-applies file edits |
| Plan mode | `--permission-mode plan` | Maps an approach, touches no files |

### The gotcha that costs teams a day: Code tab ŌēĀ legacy Claude desktop chat app

The desktop app has three tabs ŌĆö **Chat** ("general conversation with no file access, similar to claude.ai"), **Cowork** ("an autonomous background agent that works on tasks in a cloud VM," covered below), and **Code**. The **Code** tab is distinct from the *legacy Claude desktop chat app*, whose MCP configuration lives in a separate `claude_desktop_config.json` that **does not appear in the Code tab** ([desktop quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-quickstart), [issue #33732](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/33732)). So if a teammate added an MCP server to the old chat app's `claude_desktop_config.json` and wonders why the Code tab can't see it ŌĆö that's why. Code-tab MCP comes from Claude Code's own config (`.mcp.json`, user/project settings, or the Code tab's **+ ŌåÆ Plugins** UI), the *same* config the CLI reads, not from `claude_desktop_config.json`.

stage-1.heading: Surface 4 ŌĆö Cowork: the same agentic capability for non-developers
stage-1.body:
Cowork is the same agentic loop ŌĆö give it a goal, it plans, it acts across your stuff ŌĆö pointed at **non-coding knowledge work**. It is the surface you point your PMs, ops, analysts, and support leads at. The docs describe the Cowork tab as "an autonomous background agent that works on tasks in a cloud VM with its own environment" that "can run independently while you do other work"; the product page frames it as agentic non-coding knowledge work that shows its plan before acting, available on paid plans including Team and Enterprise ([Cowork product page](https://claude.com/product/cowork), [support article](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/13345190)).

### What it does

Hand off a task, get a polished deliverable: organizing file systems, building spreadsheets, generating reports, synthesizing scattered notes, scheduling recurring tasks. The defining property for trust: **before Claude acts, it shows you the plan and waits for your approval** ŌĆö you can redirect, refine, or change approach at any step. This is the difference between "an agent that ran amok in my Drive" and "an agent I supervised."

### How it reaches your tools

Cowork works across apps using a **connector for Slack, Chrome for web research, or your screen** to drive apps that have no direct integration. You keep access controls over which folders and connectors it may use. It is one of the three desktop tabs and, because it runs as an autonomous background agent in a cloud VM, it continues while you do other work; mobile can initiate tasks via phone pairing.

### Why this belongs on the menu even for an engineering org

Your infra is not only for developers. The exact same capability that fixes a failing test can reconcile a vendor spreadsheet or compile a weekly status report from Slack threads ŌĆö and if your non-engineers never learn that Cowork exists, they keep doing that work by hand while paying for the agent that would do it. Enterprise admins can manage feature access, control spend, and track Cowork usage across the org, so it slots into the same governance story as Code.

### Adjacent preview: Dispatch + computer use (know the boundary)

Computer use (Claude points/clicks/navigates your screen) and phone **Dispatch** (assign tasks from your phone) are a **research preview available for Claude Pro and Max** on macOS and Windows ([Dispatch + computer use](https://claude.com/blog/dispatch-and-computer-use), [support](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12138966)). The docs name Pro and Max; they do **not** document Team/Enterprise inclusion or exclusion ŌĆö so the accurate statement to your org is *"computer use/Dispatch is not part of the documented Team plan,"* an inference, not a stated exclusion. Treat it as experimental: it "won't always work perfectly" and is slower than a direct integration. Don't build a team workflow on it yet ŌĆö prefer a real connector where one exists.

stage-1.heading: Surface 5 ŌĆö Codex: the second coding agent (different vendor, different tree)
stage-1.body:
Codex is **OpenAI's coding agent** ŌĆö a genuinely separate product some of your devs will prefer and run alongside Claude Code. This is the one menu item that is *not* the same engine, and the most important thing to internalize is the boundary: **Codex has its own config tree (`~/.codex`); nothing syncs automatically between Codex and Claude Code** ([Codex config reference](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference), [Claude `.claude` directory](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-directory)).

### The config tree

```
~/.codex/                         # = $CODEX_HOME
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ config.toml                   # user-level config (TOML, not JSON)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ auth.json                     # CLI credentials
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ AGENTS.md                     # global agent instructions (or AGENTS.override.md)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ log/                          # logs (log_dir)
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ history.jsonl                 # session history (history.persistence)
```

(The config reference documents `config.toml`, `auth.json`, the `log/` directory, and `history.jsonl`; treat any other files you see in `~/.codex` as implementation detail, not contract.) Project-scoped overrides go in a `.codex/config.toml` in the repo (these cannot override machine-local provider/auth). The user-level config lives at `~/.codex/config.toml`; keys look like:

```toml
model = "gpt-5.5"
model_provider = "openai"           # provider ID from model_providers
approval_policy = "on-request"      # untrusted | on-request | never
sandbox_mode = "workspace-write"    # read-only | workspace-write | danger-full-access

[mcp_servers.my_server]
command = "python -m my_mcp_server"
```

Contrast with Claude Code: JSON `settings.json`, `.mcp.json`, `~/.claude/`. **Two different formats, two different trees.** A teammate who configured an MCP server for Claude Code has done *nothing* for Codex, and vice versa.

### Headless Codex

Codex's non-interactive entrypoint is `codex exec` (short form `codex e`) ŌĆö per the docs, "for scripted or CI-style runs that should finish without human interaction." The current guidance: prefer explicit `--sandbox workspace-write` over the deprecated `--full-auto` (Codex prints a warning when `--full-auto` is used), and use `approval_policy = "never"` for non-interactive runs (`on-request` for interactive) ([Codex CLI reference](https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli/reference), [config reference](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-reference)).

```bash
codex exec --sandbox workspace-write "run the tests and fix failures"
```

### The instruction-file split that bites every mixed team

This is the single most consequential interop fact:

- **Codex reads `AGENTS.md`** for project instructions, building a chain from `~/.codex/AGENTS.md` (global) down through the git root to your cwd, concatenated root-down so closer files win ([Codex AGENTS.md](https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md)).
- **Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, NOT `AGENTS.md`** ŌĆö the memory docs state verbatim: "Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md`" ([Claude memory](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory)).

So a repo with only `AGENTS.md` is invisible to Claude Code, and a repo with only `CLAUDE.md` is invisible to Codex. You **bridge** them (next section).

stage-1.heading: Bridging CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md (the interop you must set up)
stage-1.body:
If any part of your team runs Codex, you must make one set of instructions feed both agents ŌĆö otherwise the two drift and reviewers can't tell which agent saw what. There are two sanctioned bridges; both come straight from the official memory docs, which include this exact guidance: "If your repository already uses `AGENTS.md` for other coding agents, create a `CLAUDE.md` that imports it so both tools read the same instructions without duplicating them."

### Option A ŌĆö import (recommended, cross-platform)

Make `CLAUDE.md` import `AGENTS.md` with the `@path` import syntax, then add any Claude-specific notes below. Claude loads the imported file at session start, then appends the rest:

```markdown
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code
Use plan mode for changes under `src/billing/`.
```

This keeps `AGENTS.md` as the shared source of truth and works on Windows (no symlink privileges needed). Imports can recurse up to four hops; to *mention* a path without importing it, wrap it in backticks.

### Option B ŌĆö symlink (only if you need no Claude-specific content)

The docs say "A symlink also works if you don't need to add Claude-specific content":

```bash
ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md
```

On Windows a symlink "requires Administrator privileges or Developer Mode, so use the `@AGENTS.md` import instead."

### Option C ŌĆö generated mirror (what mature teams do)

Keep `CLAUDE.md` canonical and **generate** `AGENTS.md` from it (or vice versa) with a script in CI, so neither drifts. This repo's parent ecosystem does exactly this: per the project rules, *"`CLAUDE.md` is canonical per scope. `AGENTS.md` is the Codex-facing mirror... generated from CLAUDE.md via `scripts/meta/render_agents_md.py`."* The tradeoff vs. the import: a generated mirror lets you transform/filter content per-agent and gives both files real on-disk presence (some tooling greps for one or the other), at the cost of a build step that must run before commit.

### Decision

| Situation | Bridge |
|---|---|
| Mixed Codex+Claude team, want one source, Windows present | `@AGENTS.md` import in `CLAUDE.md` |
| Identical instructions, no per-agent content, macOS/Linux | symlink |
| Want per-agent transforms / both files materialized / enforce in CI | generated mirror |

A useful bonus, confirmed in the docs: running Claude Code's `/init` in a repo that already has `AGENTS.md` "reads it and incorporates the relevant parts into the generated `CLAUDE.md`. It also reads other tool configs like `.cursorrules`, `.devin/rules/`, and `.windsurfrules`."

stage-1.heading: Claude Code vs Codex ŌĆö when to reach for which
stage-1.body:
This is **not** either/or. The productive pattern reported across hundreds of developers is to run both, and assign work by their respective strengths ([Claude Code vs Codex, 2026](https://dev.to/_46ea277e677b888e0cd13/claude-code-vs-codex-2026-what-500-reddit-developers-really-think-31pb)).

### The two positions, stated fairly

- **Claude-quality.** Claude Code wins blind quality comparisons (~67% win rate cited), leads SWE-bench, and has full MCP support ŌĆö but ships tighter usage limits.
- **Codex-efficiency.** Codex is cited as cheaper (~4x fewer tokens), leads Terminal-Bench, and the community shorthand is *"Codex for keystroke, Claude Code for commits."*

Read that shorthand literally as a workflow: use the cheaper/faster agent for high-frequency, low-stakes keystroke-level help (quick edits, exploration where token cost dominates), and bring the higher-quality agent in for the consequential unit of work ŌĆö the commit, the refactor, the PR you'll actually land.

### Decision table

| Need | Reach for |
|---|---|
| Highest single-shot quality on a hard change | Claude Code |
| Full MCP tool ecosystem in the agent loop | Claude Code |
| Lowest token cost for high-volume edits | Codex |
| Terminal-task-heavy automation | Codex |
| Visual diff review + parallel worktrees + PR auto-merge | Claude Code Desktop |
| Non-developer cross-app knowledge work | Cowork |
| Runs on Linux CI with no display | Claude Code CLI (`-p`) or `codex exec` |

### What this means for your infra

Budget for both. Keep instructions bridged so the two agents share project context. Don't try to standardize the org onto one coding agent for ideological reasons ŌĆö let devs pick per-task, and make sure your `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` bridge means *the choice is free* (no re-documenting per agent). Treat the cost/win-rate figures as perishable community benchmarks, not contracts ŌĆö re-check before you set hard policy on them.

stage-1.heading: "Which one do I open?" ŌĆö the routing answers
stage-1.body:
Give your team this routing table verbatim. It answers the literal question that opened this stage.

| If you are... and you want to... | Open |
|---|---|
| A dev, iterating with full keyboard control, scripting | **CLI** (`claude`) |
| A dev, want visual diffs / parallel worktrees / live preview / PR auto-merge | **Desktop Code tab** |
| A dev or platform eng, wiring into CI, git hooks, a linter, a cron | **Headless** (`claude --bare -p ...`) |
| A PM/ops/analyst, want a deliverable across files + Slack + web | **Cowork** |
| A dev who prefers OpenAI's agent or wants cheap high-volume edits | **Codex** (`codex` / `codex exec`) |
| On Linux (laptop, server, CI runner) | **CLI / headless** only (no Desktop) |

### Cross-surface continuity

Because the three Claude Code surfaces share an engine and config, you can **start in one and continue in another**. A specific CLI session can be resumed by session id with `--resume`, and the docs note the lookup "is scoped to the current project directory and its git worktrees." The Desktop app can send long-running work to the cloud or continue a session on the web/IDE. The portability is real *because* the config is shared ŌĆö which is the whole reason to model these as surfaces, not separate tools.

### Headless continuation example

```bash
session_id=$(claude -p "Start a review" --output-format json | jq -r '.session_id')
claude -p "Continue that review" --resume "$session_id"
```

Run both commands from the same directory ŌĆö session-id lookup is scoped to the current project directory and its git worktrees. (To just pick up the most recent conversation without capturing an id, use `--continue` instead of `--resume`.)

stage-1.heading: Config surface map ŌĆö where each thing actually reads from
stage-1.body:
Picking the right surface also means knowing *which files it reads*, so you don't edit config that the surface in front of you ignores.

### Claude Code (CLI, Desktop Code tab, headless) ŌĆö shared stack

Load order is broadŌåÆspecific (managed ŌåÆ user ŌåÆ project ŌåÆ local); files closer to your cwd are read last and win:

```
/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md             # managed policy (Linux/WSL)
/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md   # managed policy (macOS)
C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md  # managed policy (Windows)
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md                    # user-level instructions (all projects)
~/.claude/settings.json                # user settings
~/.claude/rules/*.md                   # user-level rules
<repo>/CLAUDE.md  or  .claude/CLAUDE.md # project instructions (committed)
<repo>/CLAUDE.local.md                  # personal project prefs (gitignore)
<repo>/.claude/rules/*.md               # project rules (optional paths: frontmatter)
<repo>/.mcp.json                        # project MCP servers
<repo>/.claude/settings.json            # project settings
<repo>/.claude/settings.local.json      # personal/local settings
```

`CLAUDE.md` and `CLAUDE.local.md` files in the directory hierarchy load *in full* at launch; within each directory `CLAUDE.local.md` is appended after `CLAUDE.md`. Auto memory is separate: it lives at `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md`, and only its first 200 lines / 25KB load at session start. Path-scoped rules use frontmatter:

```markdown
---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
# API rules (load only when touching matching files)
```

### Codex ŌĆö separate stack

```
~/.codex/config.toml      # user config (TOML)
~/.codex/AGENTS.md        # global instructions (or AGENTS.override.md)
<repo>/.codex/config.toml # project config override
<repo>/AGENTS.md          # project instructions (chained git-root ŌåÆ cwd, closer wins)
```

### Legacy Claude desktop chat app ŌĆö a third, unrelated stack

```
claude_desktop_config.json   # MCP for the OLD chat app ONLY
                             # NOT read by the Code tab or the CLI
```

Keep this triple-tree picture posted. Most "my config isn't working" tickets are someone editing a file the surface in front of them doesn't read.

stage-1.heading: Common pitfalls and gotchas
stage-1.body:
### 1. Editing `claude_desktop_config.json` and expecting the Code tab to see it
That file is the *legacy chat app's* MCP config. The Code tab (and CLI) read Claude Code's own config (`.mcp.json`, settings). Symptom: "I added the MCP server but the agent can't use it." Fix: add it to `.mcp.json` or via the Code tab's **+ ŌåÆ Plugins** UI ([issue #33732](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/33732)).

### 2. Expecting headless in the Desktop GUI
There is no `-p` in the GUI; the Desktop app is interactive only. Anything that must run unattended (CI, hooks, cron-without-the-app) is CLI/headless territory ([headless docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless)). The Desktop *can* run scheduled tasks, but that's the app driving an interactive session, not a Unix-pipe headless call.

### 3. Headless run aborts on a tool prompt
No human to approve ŌåÆ an un-pre-approved tool kills the run. Pre-grant with `--allowedTools` or a `--permission-mode`. And mind the space in `Bash(git diff *)` ŌĆö drop it and you over-match (`git diff-index`). Note `--permission-mode acceptEdits` auto-approves edits and common fs commands but *not* arbitrary shell/network ŌĆö those still need an `--allowedTools` entry.

### 4. `CLAUDE.md` Ōåö `AGENTS.md` drift
Claude Code does **not** read `AGENTS.md`; Codex does **not** read `CLAUDE.md`. A repo with only one is half-blind to the team. Bridge with `@AGENTS.md` import, a symlink, or a generated mirror ŌĆö and pick *one* canonical file so they can't disagree.

### 5. Assuming Codex inherits your Claude config
Nothing syncs. Different tree (`~/.codex`), different format (TOML vs JSON), different MCP block (`[mcp_servers.<id>]` vs `.mcp.json`). Configure both, or you'll have one dev whose MCP tools "don't exist" in the other agent.

### 6. Non-reproducible CI because of ambient config
Without `--bare`, `claude -p` loads *whatever* is in the runner's `~/.claude` and the repo's `.mcp.json`/hooks ŌĆö so a teammate's hook can change your build. Use `--bare` plus explicit `--settings`/`--mcp-config` for deterministic CI. Remember bare mode needs `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or an `apiKeyHelper` (no keychain/OAuth).

### 7. Treating computer use / Dispatch as production
Research preview, Pro/Max-documented, macOS/Windows, "won't always work perfectly," slower than a direct integration. Don't put it on a team-critical path; prefer a real connector/integration where one exists.

### 8. Pointing non-developers at the Code tab
The Code-tab review loop is built around *diffs and git*. For an analyst reconciling spreadsheets, that's the wrong surface ŌĆö Cowork (goal in, plan shown, deliverable out, works across Slack/Chrome/screen) is the one built for them. Get this wrong and your ops people either bounce off a developer tool or never adopt anything at all.

stage-2.heading: The bug isn't in the prompt ŌĆö it's in the room
stage-2.body:
Two engineers paste the same prompt ŌĆö "add input validation to the billing endpoint" ŌĆö into the same repo. One gets code with Pydantic models, structured logging, and a test. The other gets a bare `if not x: return`. Same model, same prompt, same files. The difference is everything the agent loaded *before* the prompt: one teammate has a `CLAUDE.md` that says "API handlers validate with Pydantic and log all state changes," a `.claude/settings.json` that denies `Bash(git push:*)` so the agent doesn't surprise-push, and a skill that knows the house test command. The other teammate is running bare.

This is the central insight of this stage: **an agent's behavior is a function of its configuration, and configuration is usually invisible.** Nobody reviews it, nobody versions it, nobody diffs it. When the prompt is the only artifact you share, you're sharing the smallest, least important part of the setup. The fix is to treat the *setup itself* ŌĆö instructions, permissions, hooks, skills ŌĆö as code: one canonical source, committed, reviewed in PRs, and projected to every engine your team uses (Claude Code and Codex) so switching tools doesn't mean re-teaching each one.

The rest of this stage is the concrete machinery: what `CLAUDE.md` and `AGENTS.md` are and how to keep them from diverging, what lives in `.claude/`, how `settings.json` permissions and precedence actually evaluate, and the failure modes that bite teams at scale. Throughout, keep one line from the official docs in mind, because it draws the sharpest boundary in the whole stage: "Settings rules are enforced by the client regardless of what Claude decides to do. CLAUDE.md instructions shape Claude's behavior but are not a hard enforcement layer."

stage-2.heading: CLAUDE.md: the always-loaded instruction file
stage-2.body:
`CLAUDE.md` is a plain-markdown file Claude Code loads into context at the **start of every session**. It is not a system prompt and not enforced configuration ŌĆö the docs are explicit: "CLAUDE.md content is delivered as a user message after the system prompt, not as part of the system prompt itself. Claude reads it and tries to follow it, but there's no guarantee of strict compliance." It *shapes* what the agent tries to do; it does not *guarantee* anything. (For hard guarantees you need hooks and permission rules ŌĆö covered below.)

### What goes in it
The load-bearing content is the stuff you'd otherwise re-explain every session. The docs frame the trigger as: add to `CLAUDE.md` when "Claude makes the same mistake a second time," "a code review catches something Claude should have known," or "a new teammate would need the same context."
- Build/test commands (`npm run check`, `make test`)
- Project layout ("API handlers live in `src/api/handlers/`")
- Conventions and "always do X" rules
- Architecture decisions and gotchas a new teammate would need

If an entry is a multi-step *procedure* or only matters for one part of the tree, the docs say to move it to a skill or a path-scoped rule instead ŌĆö not to grow `CLAUDE.md`.

### Where it can live (load order, broad ŌåÆ specific)
| Scope | Location | Shared with |
|---|---|---|
| Managed policy | `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md` (macOS), `/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md` (Linux/WSL), `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md` (Windows) | All users on machine (IT-deployed; cannot be excluded) |
| User | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Just you, every project |
| Project | `./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md` | The team, via git |
| Local | `./CLAUDE.local.md` (gitignore it) | Just you, this repo |

Claude walks **up** the directory tree from your cwd and concatenates every `CLAUDE.md` and `CLAUDE.local.md` it finds. Content is ordered root-first ("from the filesystem root down to your working directory"), so instructions closest to where you launched are read **last**; within a directory, `CLAUDE.local.md` is appended after `CLAUDE.md`. Subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` files load **on demand** when Claude reads files there ŌĆö this is the "context cascade" that makes per-package instructions work without bloating every session.

### Specificity is the whole game
The docs measure adherence by specificity. "Use 2-space indentation" beats "Format code properly"; "Run `npm test` before committing" beats "Test your changes"; "API handlers live in `src/api/handlers/`" beats "Keep files organized." Write instructions concrete enough to *verify*. Vague rules and ŌĆö worse ŌĆö *contradictory* rules across nested files cause Claude to "pick one arbitrarily."

stage-2.heading: Keep CLAUDE.md short, owned, and reviewed
stage-2.body:
The dominant anti-pattern at scale is the **over-stuffed, unowned `CLAUDE.md`** ŌĆö a 600-line file nobody owns, everyone appends to, and that quietly contradicts itself. Two reasons this is corrosive:

1. **Context cost.** `CLAUDE.md` loads *in full* every session regardless of length (the docs: "CLAUDE.md files are loaded in full regardless of length, though shorter files produce better adherence"). The docs set an explicit target: **target under 200 lines per file**. Longer files "consume more context and reduce adherence" ŌĆö the irony being that the more you cram in to control the agent, the *less* reliably it follows any of it.
2. **Rot.** An unowned file accumulates stale rules. When two rules conflict, behavior becomes nondeterministic ŌĆö "Claude may pick one arbitrarily."

### The three disciplines
- **Give it an owner.** Someone is responsible for pruning. The test from the docs: add a line when "Claude makes the same mistake a second time" or "a new teammate would need the same context" ŌĆö not speculatively.
- **Review it like code.** Changes to `CLAUDE.md` go through PR review. A new "always do X" rule is a behavioral change to every agent session on the team; it deserves the same scrutiny as a code change.
- **Cascade instead of cramming.** Use a root `CLAUDE.md` for project-wide facts and **`.claude/rules/`** (or per-package files) for the local stuff. Rules go in `.claude/rules/<topic>.md`; a rule *without* a `paths:` frontmatter loads every session (same priority as `.claude/CLAUDE.md`), while a **path-scoped** rule loads only when Claude touches matching files:

```markdown
---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
# API rules
- All API endpoints must include input validation
- Use the standard error response format
```

A path-scoped rule costs zero context until the agent opens a matching file. That's how you keep the always-loaded surface small while still encoding deep, local knowledge. (`paths` accepts glob patterns and brace expansion: `"src/**/*.{ts,tsx}"`.)

### Imports for organization
`CLAUDE.md` can pull in other files with `@path/to/file` syntax. Relative paths resolve against the *importing* file (not cwd); imports recurse to a **maximum depth of four hops**. Caveat from the docs: imported files "are expanded and loaded into context at launch," so imports buy *organization*, not context savings ŌĆö "Splitting into `@path` imports helps organization but does not reduce context." To mention a path without importing it, wrap it in backticks (`` `@README` ``). The first time a project uses external imports, Claude shows a one-time approval dialog listing the files.

### One thing the docs preserve
Block-level HTML comments (`<!-- maintainer notes -->`) are stripped before injection, so you can leave human-only notes that cost zero context. Project-root `CLAUDE.md` is also re-read and re-injected after `/compact` ŌĆö nested subdirectory files are not, so anything that must survive compaction belongs at the root (or in a hook).

stage-2.heading: AGENTS.md: the Codex-facing mirror
stage-2.body:
`AGENTS.md` is the same idea ŌĆö a markdown file giving AI coding agents project context ŌĆö but it's the **open standard** the rest of the ecosystem reads. Per agents.md it is "stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation," "used by over 60k open-source projects," and read by **25+ tools** including OpenAI Codex, Google Jules, Aider, goose, Cursor, Gemini CLI, VS Code, Devin, and GitHub Copilot's coding agent. The format is deliberately minimal: "AGENTS.md is just standard Markdown. Use any headings you like; the agent simply parses the text you provide." There are no required fields.

For monorepos, AGENTS.md uses **closest-file-wins**: "The closest AGENTS.md to the edited file wins; explicit user chat prompts override everything." Each subproject ships tailored instructions ŌĆö the same cascade idea as nested `CLAUDE.md`.

### How Codex actually reads it
Codex's discovery is concrete and worth knowing, because it shapes how you place files. Per the Codex docs, Codex reads, in order, from your Codex home (`~/.codex` unless `CODEX_HOME` is set) then from the project root down to cwd, checking each directory for `AGENTS.override.md`, then `AGENTS.md`, then fallback names. It "concatenates files from the root down, joining them with blank lines," and "files closer to your current directory override earlier guidance because they appear later in the combined prompt." Instructions are capped at `project_doc_max_bytes` (**32 KiB by default**) before truncation.

### The asymmetry that creates the whole problem
Here is the fact that drives this stage's design, and it's easy to get wrong:

> **Claude Code reads ONLY `CLAUDE.md`. It does not read `AGENTS.md` ŌĆö not even as a fallback.** The docs say it directly: "Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md`."

Codex reads `AGENTS.md`. Claude reads `CLAUDE.md`. They do not natively share a file. So a team that uses both engines ŌĆö or even a single dev who switches between Claude Code and Codex in VS Code ŌĆö has two instruction files that *will* drift unless you do something about it. (Note: the agents.md tool list does **not** include Claude Code; trust Claude's own docs here ŌĆö the native reader is `CLAUDE.md`, and the bridge below is how Claude participates in the standard.)

That "something" is the config mirror.

stage-2.heading: The config mirror: one canonical source, projected
stage-2.body:
The principle: **keep ONE canonical config and project it to the other engine** rather than hand-maintaining two files that silently fall out of sync. There are two mechanically different ways to bridge `CLAUDE.md` Ōåö `AGENTS.md`, and the choice matters for cross-platform teams.

### Option A ŌĆö the `@AGENTS.md` import (preferred, portable)
Make `AGENTS.md` the canonical file (it's the open standard, the broader-reach format) and have `CLAUDE.md` import it. This is the exact pattern the Claude docs recommend:

```markdown CLAUDE.md
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code
Use plan mode for changes under `src/billing/`.
```

Claude "loads the imported file at session start, then appends the rest." This works on **Windows with no admin rights**, recurses up to four hops, and lets you add engine-specific notes below the import. This is the recommended bridge.

### Option B ŌĆö the symlink
If you don't need Claude-specific content, symlink the two ŌĆö also shown in the Claude docs:

```bash
ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md
```

Now both engines read the same bytes. The catch, stated in the docs: "On Windows, creating a symlink requires Administrator privileges or Developer Mode, so use the `@AGENTS.md` import instead." (Mixed-OS teams hit a second snag: git only materializes the symlink as a real link when `core.symlinks` is enabled on the Windows clone; otherwise it checks out as a plain text file containing the path string.) Use the symlink only on all-POSIX teams.

### Comparison
| | `@AGENTS.md` import | `ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md` symlink |
|---|---|---|
| Canonical file | `AGENTS.md` | `AGENTS.md` |
| Windows without admin | Works | Needs Admin / Developer Mode |
| Add Claude-only content | Yes, below the import | No (identical bytes) |
| Recursion / nesting | Up to 4 hops | N/A |
| Risk of silent drift | Low (single source) | None (literally one file) |

### The generated-projection variant
A third pattern, used in this very ecosystem, treats `CLAUDE.md` (or a `relationships.yaml`) as canonical and **deterministically renders** `AGENTS.md` from it ŌĆö see `project-meta/scripts/meta/render_agents_md.py`, which extracts a fixed set of sections "without making `AGENTS.md` a second hand-maintained authority." Use this when the two files need different *shapes* (e.g., Codex wants a flattened projection, or you want only some sections mirrored), not just different bytes. Whatever you pick, the rule is the same: **exactly one file is authored by hand; the other is derived.**

stage-2.heading: The drift check: fail CI when the mirror diverges
stage-2.body:
A mirror is only as good as the thing that catches it breaking. The discipline that makes "configure once" real is a **drift check in CI** that fails the build when the canonical source and its projection diverge. Without it, the import or symlink is a *convention*, and conventions rot.

This ecosystem ships exactly such a check: `project-meta/scripts/check_agent_surface_sync.py`. It is "intentionally read-only" and "reports whether the workspace root instruction files resolve to the same content and whether Claude/Codex skill discovery directories have obvious mirror drift." The mechanism is a content hash: render the projection, hash it, compare to the committed file.

### What a drift gate looks like in CI
```yaml
# .github/workflows/agent-config.yml
name: agent-config
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  surface-sync:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - name: Re-render AGENTS.md and fail on diff
        run: python scripts/meta/render_agents_md.py --check
      - name: Hash CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md + skill dirs
        run: python scripts/check_agent_surface_sync.py
```

The `--check` mode re-renders the projection and exits non-zero if it differs from what's committed ŌĆö the same pattern as `black --check` or `prettier --check`. A reviewer who edits `AGENTS.md` but forgets to regenerate (or edits the wrong file) gets a red build, not a teammate three days later wondering why Codex and Claude disagree.

### Why this is the linchpin
The payoff is concrete: a dev can switch between Claude Code and Codex in VS Code and get **identical rules**, because CI guarantees the two surfaces can't silently diverge. The drift check is what turns "we have two instruction files" from a liability into a single logical source with two physical projections. Note one real limit: if you use the bare `@AGENTS.md` import, `CLAUDE.md` legitimately carries *extra* Claude-only lines below the import ŌĆö so the check must compare the *resolved/imported* content, not byte-for-byte, or scope the hash to the shared region. A pure symlink sidesteps this (one file, nothing to diff).

stage-2.heading: The .claude/ directory: configuration as committed code
stage-2.body:
`CLAUDE.md` is instructions. The `.claude/` directory is everything *else* an agent needs, stored as **versioned code in the repo**. The decisive fact:

> A committed `.claude/` directory **travels with the repo**. Cloning it (and accepting the workspace-trust dialog) gives a teammate the project's skills, agents, hooks, and `settings.json`. (`code.claude.com/docs/en/skills`, `/settings`)

That single sentence is what makes setup shareable. The teammate who clones doesn't configure anything ŌĆö they accept one trust prompt and inherit the whole team's agent setup.

### Layout
```text
your-project/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ CLAUDE.md                    # always-loaded instructions (or @AGENTS.md import)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ AGENTS.md                    # canonical, Codex-facing
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .claude/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ settings.json            # committed: permissions, hooks, env (team)
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ settings.local.json      # gitignored: personal overrides
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ rules/                   # instruction files (path-scoped or always-on)
    Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ testing.md
    Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ api-design.md
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/                  # reusable procedures (SKILL.md per skill)
    Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ run-migrations/
    Ōöé       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ SKILL.md
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ agents/                  # subagent definitions
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ reviewer.md
```

### What's shared vs. personal
- **Committed** (`.claude/settings.json`, `rules/`, `skills/`, `agents/`): the team contract. Reviewed in PRs.
- **Gitignored** (`.claude/settings.local.json`, `CLAUDE.local.md`): personal overrides ŌĆö your sandbox URLs, your extra allow-rules, your preferred model. These layer *on top* of the shared config without forcing your preferences on the team.

The trust dialog matters and is load-bearing: because `.claude/` can contain hooks (arbitrary shell commands) and tool-granting skills, Claude Code gates a freshly-cloned `.claude/` behind a one-time workspace-trust prompt. The docs tie multiple capabilities to that gate ŌĆö a project skill's `allowed-tools` "takes effect after you accept the workspace trust dialog for that folder, the same as permission rules in `.claude/settings.json`," and `autoMemoryDirectory` set in project settings is "honored only after you accept the workspace trust dialog." That's the deliberate friction that keeps a malicious repo from auto-executing a hook or self-granting broad tool access on clone ŌĆö accept it knowingly, the same way you'd review a `Makefile` before running `make`.

stage-2.heading: settings.json: permissions, hooks, and env
Permission rules: syntax and the denyŌåÆaskŌåÆallow order
stage-2.body:
Permission rules are the safety surface, so the exact semantics matter. A rule is `Tool` or `Tool(specifier)`.

### Specifier syntax
| Rule | Effect |
|---|---|
| `Bash` (bare) | Matches *all* Bash; as a deny rule it "removes the tool from Claude's context entirely" |
| `Bash(npm run build)` | Exact command match |
| `Bash(npm run test:*)` | Trailing wildcard ŌĆö `:*` at the end is "equivalent to a trailing ` *`" (enforces a word boundary) |
| `Read(.env)` / `Read(**/.env)` | Equivalent; any `.env` at or under cwd (gitignore semantics, matches at any depth) |
| `Read(/src/**)` | Project-root-relative ŌĆö note `/path` is the **project root**, NOT the filesystem root (`//path` is absolute) |
| `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` | Fetch requests to that hostname |
| `mcp__puppeteer__*` | All tools from the `puppeteer` MCP server |

The space before `*` is load-bearing: `Bash(ls *)` matches `ls -la` but **not** `lsof`; `Bash(ls*)` matches both. Claude Code is shell-aware ŌĆö `Bash(safe-cmd *)` does **not** authorize `safe-cmd && rm -rf .`, because "a rule must match each subcommand independently" (recognized separators: `&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`, `|&`, `&`, newlines). It also strips a fixed set of process wrappers before matching, so `Bash(npm test *)` also matches `timeout 30 npm test` (wrappers: `timeout`, `time`, `nice`, `nohup`, `stdbuf`, and bare `xargs`).

### Evaluation order (memorize this)
From the docs verbatim: "Rules are evaluated in order: deny, then ask, then allow. The first match in that order determines the outcome, and rule specificity does not change the order." Consequences:
- A broad `deny` like `Bash(aws *)` blocks *everything* matching, "including calls that also match a narrower allow rule like `Bash(aws s3 ls)`, so a deny rule cannot carry allowlist exceptions."
- "A matching ask rule prompts even when a more specific allow rule also matches."
- Permission rules are "enforced by Claude Code, not by the model" ŌĆö so unlike `CLAUDE.md`, a deny rule is a real wall.

### Fragile-pattern gotcha
Don't try to constrain command *arguments* with Bash patterns ŌĆö the docs flag this as fragile by design: `Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)` is trivially bypassed by `curl -X GET ŌĆ”`, `https://`, redirects, or a shell variable. There's a hard version of this too: a rule like `Bash(command:rm *)` (param-matching on `command`) is "bypassable by a compound command, so Claude Code ignores it and emits a startup warning." For URL control the docs prescribe: **deny `curl`/`wget` entirely and use `WebFetch(domain:ŌĆ”)` allow-rules**, or enforce with a `PreToolUse` hook. Note `WebFetch` alone does not block the network ŌĆö if Bash is allowed, Claude can still curl anywhere. Watch one more env-runner trap: `Bash(devbox run *)` (or `npx`, `docker exec`) matches *whatever comes after `run`*, including `devbox run rm -rf .`, because those runners aren't on the stripped-wrapper list ŌĆö write `Bash(devbox run npm test)` instead.

stage-2.heading: Settings precedence: merge vs. override, and the deny floor
stage-2.body:
Settings come from multiple scopes, and the merge rules differ by setting type ŌĆö this trips up teams constantly.

### Precedence for most settings (override, highest wins per key)
From the docs, highest to lowest:
1. **Managed settings** ŌĆö "cannot be overridden by any other level, including command line arguments"
2. **Command-line arguments** ŌĆö temporary session overrides
3. **Local** (`.claude/settings.local.json`)
4. **Project** (`.claude/settings.json`)
5. **User** (`~/.claude/settings.json`)

So your personal `settings.local.json` overrides the committed project `settings.json` for scalar keys like `model` ŌĆö which is exactly what you want for personal overrides that don't touch the team. (Managed `settings.json` lives in `managed-settings.json` at `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/`, `/etc/claude-code/`, or `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\` ŌĆö a *different* filename from the managed `CLAUDE.md`.)

### Permission rules are the exception (merge)
The `allow`/`ask`/`deny` arrays **concatenate across all scopes** rather than override. The critical consequence, stated verbatim in the docs:

> "If a tool is denied at any level, no other level can allow it. For example, a managed settings deny cannot be overridden by `--allowedTools`ŌĆ” a user-level deny blocks a project-level allow, because deny rules from any scope are evaluated before allow rules."
