A higher-scope **deny is a floor no lower scope can relax.** This is a feature: an org can deploy a managed `deny` for `Read(./.env)` and *no* engineer's local settings can re-enable it. You build security *up* by adding deny rules at higher scopes, and you can't accidentally weaken them from below. (To go further, managed settings can set `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly: true`, which makes *only* managed allow/ask/deny rules apply at all.)

### Worked example
| Scope | Rule | Result for `git push origin main` |
|---|---|---|
| Project `settings.json` | `allow: ["Bash(git push:*)"]` | would allowŌĆ” |
| User `settings.json` | `deny: ["Bash(git push * main)"]` | ŌĆ”but deny is evaluated first ŌåÆ **blocked** |

Deny-first + merge means the user's deny beats the project's allow. The agent cannot push to main no matter what the repo's committed settings say.

stage-2.heading: Hooks and skills: the rest of what travels in .claude/
stage-2.body:
Two more `.claude/` surfaces round out the shared setup, because committing them is what makes the *whole* workflow reproducible ŌĆö not just the rules.

### Hooks ŌĆö enforcement and automation
Hooks are shell commands wired to lifecycle events in `settings.json`. They run *as the harness*, not as the model, so they're how you enforce things the model can't be trusted to do every time:
- `PreToolUse` ŌĆö runs before a tool call; **exit code 2 blocks it**, and the docs are explicit that this "stops the tool call before permission rules are evaluated," overriding even an allow rule. This is the "block edits to protected files" pattern.
- `SessionStart` / `SessionEnd` ŌĆö bootstrap env, emit telemetry.

One subtlety worth knowing: a `PreToolUse` hook can also *return* `"allow"`/`"ask"` decisions, but those "do not bypass permission rules" ŌĆö a matching deny or ask rule still fires. So hooks can tighten or auto-approve, but they cannot loosen a deny. Because hooks execute arbitrary commands, they're exactly why the clone trust-dialog exists.

### Skills ŌĆö reusable procedures, loaded on demand
A skill is a folder under `.claude/skills/<name>/` with a `SKILL.md` whose YAML frontmatter declares when it should fire. Per the docs, "Every skill needs a `SKILL.md` file with two parts: YAML frontmatter between `---` markers that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with the instructions." Unlike `CLAUDE.md` (always loaded in full), a skill's *description* sits in context but its **body loads only when invoked or judged relevant**, so it costs almost no baseline context:

```markdown
---
name: run-migrations
description: Run and verify DB migrations. Use when the user adds a model field or mentions Alembic/schema changes.
allowed-tools: Bash(make migrate-gen *) Bash(make migrate-up)
---
# Run migrations
1. `make migrate-gen MSG="<change>"`
2. Review the generated migration
3. `make migrate-up` and confirm the schema diff
```

The directory name (`run-migrations/`) becomes the `/run-migrations` command; `description` is what Claude reads to decide when to auto-load (and is truncated at 1,536 characters in the listing, so put the key use case first). Only `description` is recommended; all other frontmatter fields are optional. Skills follow the Agent Skills open standard (agentskills.io). Committing skills means the procedure your senior engineer figured out once is available to every teammate's agent automatically. To mirror skills onto Codex's surface, the ecosystem pattern is a **portable source** (e.g. `~/.agents/skills`) symlinked into both `.claude/skills/` and Codex's directory ŌĆö with the same drift check guarding it. This is the skill-level analogue of the `CLAUDE.md` Ōåö `AGENTS.md` mirror: one source, projected to each engine, CI-verified.

stage-2.heading: When to use what
stage-2.body:
A decision table for "where does this piece of setup belong":

| You want toŌĆ” | Put it in | Why |
|---|---|---|
| State a convention every session should know | `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` | Always-loaded behavioral guidance |
| Encode a deep rule for one package only | `.claude/rules/*.md` with `paths:` | Loads only when matching files are touched ŌĆö saves context |
| **Block** a command/path no matter what | `settings.json` `permissions.deny` (or managed) | Enforced by the client, not the model; deny is evaluated first |
| Require confirmation for risky ops | `settings.json` `permissions.ask` | e.g. `Bash(git push:*)` |
| Make a routine check automatic and unbypassable | `settings.json` `hooks` (`PreToolUse`) | Runs as the harness; exit 2 blocks before rules evaluate |
| Package a multi-step procedure | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Loads on demand, near-zero baseline cost |
| Personal override (model, sandbox URL, extra allow) | `settings.local.json` / `CLAUDE.local.md` (gitignored) | Layers on top without touching the team |
| Org-wide non-negotiable | Managed settings (`managed-settings.json`) / managed `CLAUDE.md` | Can't be relaxed or excluded by any lower scope |

### The cross-engine layer (orthogonal to the above)
For *any* of the instruction/skill surfaces: author **one** canonical file, project it to the other engine (import, symlink, or generated render), and gate it with a **CI drift check**. That's what makes the whole table true for both Claude Code and Codex simultaneously.

stage-2.heading: Common pitfalls and gotchas
stage-2.body:
### 1. The 600-line CLAUDE.md
The most common failure. It loads in full every session, costs context, and *reduces* adherence. Symptom: the agent ignores rules buried mid-file. Fix: cap at ~200 lines (the docs' explicit target), push depth into `.claude/rules/` (path-scoped) and skills, give the file an owner.

### 2. Putting enforcement in CLAUDE.md
"NEVER push to main" in `CLAUDE.md` is a *suggestion* ŌĆö it's "delivered as a user message," not a guarantee, with "no guarantee of strict compliance." The agent will mostly comply and occasionally won't. Real enforcement is `permissions.deny` or a `PreToolUse` hook. The docs say it plainly: "To block an action regardless of what Claude decides, use a PreToolUse hook instead."

### 3. Assuming Claude reads AGENTS.md
It doesn't ŌĆö *ever*, not even as a fallback ("Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md`"). A repo with only `AGENTS.md` gives Claude Code **zero** project instructions. You must bridge with `@AGENTS.md` import or a symlink, or Claude runs blind while Codex runs informed. This is the silent version of the two-teammates bug. (Also: agents.md's own tool list doesn't name Claude Code, so don't expect it to "just work.")

### 4. Symlink on Windows
`ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md` requires Admin/Developer Mode on Windows (per the docs), and git needs `core.symlinks` enabled on the clone or the link checks out as a plain text file containing the path string. On a mixed-OS team, use the `@AGENTS.md` import.

### 5. Expecting a deny exception
`deny: ["Bash(aws *)"]` + `allow: ["Bash(aws s3 ls)"]` does **not** carve out an exception ŌĆö "a deny rule cannot carry allowlist exceptions," so `aws s3 ls` is blocked. Model your deny rules as absolute floors, and remember a higher-scope deny can't be relaxed from below.

### 6. Committing secrets in settings.json env
`settings.json` is committed. `env` values land in git. Keep secrets out; use `settings.local.json` (gitignored) or real secret management.

### 7. Trying to constrain arguments with Bash patterns
`Bash(curl ŌĆ” *)` is fragile (bypassable by flags/protocol/redirects/variables), and the param form `Bash(command:rm *)` is *ignored* with a startup warning. Env-runner rules like `Bash(devbox run *)` or `Bash(npx *)` silently authorize anything after `run`. Constrain at the tool level (`WebFetch(domain:ŌĆ”)`, deny `curl`/`wget`) or with a hook ŌĆö not with argument globs.

### 8. No drift check
A mirror with no CI gate *will* diverge ŌĆö someone edits the wrong file, regenerates nothing, and three days later the two engines disagree. The drift check (`render --check` + `check_agent_surface_sync.py`) is not optional polish; it's the thing that makes "configure once" hold under a team's edit pressure.

### 9. Forgetting the clone trust dialog in onboarding
A teammate clones, the `.claude/` config is right there, but until they accept the workspace-trust prompt, hooks, project-skill `allowed-tools`, and trusted local settings (`autoMemoryDirectory`) don't activate. Document this one-time step in onboarding so new hires don't run with half the setup silently disabled.

stage-3.heading: The itch this stage scratches
stage-3.body:
You notice it the tenth time you paste the same five-step deploy checklist into chat. Or when a teammate force-pushes to `main` *again* despite the line in `CLAUDE.md` that says not to. Or when you realize half your context window is gone before you've typed a word, eaten by an MCP server you wired up last week and forgot about.

Those are distinct itches, and they map to distinct mechanisms. Getting the mapping right is the whole game:

- **"I keep re-describing a *procedure*"** ŌåÆ a **skill**. On-demand, model-invoked, loaded only when relevant.
- **"A rule must *always* hold, no matter what the model decides"** ŌåÆ a **hook**. Deterministic, event-driven, can hard-block.
- **"I want a *bounded* sub-task done without polluting my main context"** ŌåÆ a **sub-agent**. Own window, least-privilege tools, returns a summary.
- **"I need Claude to *reach into* an external system"** ŌåÆ an **MCP server**. A typed gateway to GitHub, a database, a tracker.

The failure mode is reaching for the wrong one: encoding a non-negotiable policy as advisory `CLAUDE.md` prose (it gets ignored under load), or bolting a 40-tool MCP server onto every session when a CLI call would do. This stage goes deep on each primitive, its real file format, and the decision boundary between them. Everything below is the actual on-disk format as of mid-2026 ŌĆö verified against `code.claude.com/docs`, `developers.openai.com/codex`, and `agentskills.io`, not from memory.

stage-3.heading: Skills: a capability whose description is its trigger
stage-3.body:
A **skill** is an on-demand capability packaged as a directory with a `SKILL.md` entrypoint. The defining property: **it is model-invoked via its description.** At session start Claude loads only each skill's `name` + `description` (cheap). When your request matches a description, Claude loads the full `SKILL.md` body and follows it. You can also fire it explicitly with `/skill-name`.

The docs give a one-line decision rule (code.claude.com/docs/en/skills): create a skill *"when you keep pasting the same instructions, checklist, or multi-step procedure into chat, or when a section of CLAUDE.md has grown into a procedure rather than a fact."* A fact belongs in `CLAUDE.md`; a *procedure* belongs in a skill, because the skill's body loads only when used, so long reference material costs almost nothing until you need it.

Note: in Claude Code, **custom commands have been merged into skills.** A file at `.claude/commands/deploy.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` both create `/deploy`; the skill form just adds a directory for supporting files, frontmatter for invocation control, and automatic model invocation.

### File layout

```text
.claude/skills/
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ summarize-changes/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ SKILL.md          # required entrypoint
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ reference.md      # optional, loaded only when referenced
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ scripts/
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ validate.sh    # optional, executed not loaded
```

The **directory name** becomes the `/command` you type. The frontmatter `name` is only a display label shown in listings ŌĆö *except* at a plugin root, where there is no skill directory and `name` does set the command.

### SKILL.md frontmatter

The docs' canonical first example carries only a `description` ŌĆö every other field is optional:

```yaml
---
description: Summarizes uncommitted changes and flags anything risky. Use when the user asks what changed, wants a commit message, or asks to review their diff.
---

## Current changes

!`git diff HEAD`

## Instructions

Summarize the changes above in two or three bullet points, then list any risks.
```

The `` !`git diff HEAD` `` line is **dynamic context injection**: Claude Code runs the command *before* the model sees the body and inlines the output (this is preprocessing ŌĆö Claude never executes it). For multi-line, use a ` ```! ` fenced block. The inline form only fires when `!` starts a line or follows whitespace; `KEY=!`cmd`` is left literal.

stage-3.heading: Skill frontmatter fields that actually matter
stage-3.body:
Every frontmatter field is optional; only `description` is *recommended* (without it Claude has nothing to match on, and falls back to the first paragraph of the body). Here are the fields you will actually reach for (code.claude.com/docs/en/skills, frontmatter reference):

| Field | Effect |
|-------|--------|
| `description` | The trigger. Put the key use case first. Combined `description` + `when_to_use` is truncated at **1,536 chars** in the listing (configurable via `maxSkillDescriptionChars`). |
| `when_to_use` | Extra trigger phrases / example requests, appended to `description`; counts toward the 1,536-char cap. |
| `disable-model-invocation: true` | Only *you* can invoke (`/name`); description is not even loaded into context. Use for side-effecting workflows ŌĆö `/deploy`, `/commit` ŌĆö you don't want Claude auto-running. |
| `user-invocable: false` | Hide from the `/` menu; only *Claude* invokes. Use for background knowledge that isn't a meaningful user command. |
| `allowed-tools` | Pre-approve tools while the skill is active (e.g. `Bash(git add *) Bash(git commit *)`). Does **not** restrict the pool ŌĆö it just skips the permission prompt. |
| `disallowed-tools` | *Removes* tools from the pool while the skill is active (e.g. `AskUserQuestion` for a background loop). Clears on your next message. |
| `context: fork` + `agent` | Run the skill in a sub-agent's isolated context (see the sub-agent section). |
| `model` / `effort` | Override the model or effort level for the rest of the turn while the skill is active. |
| `argument-hint` / `arguments` | Autocomplete hint and named `$arg` substitution. |
| `paths` | Glob patterns; auto-activate the skill only when working on matching files. |

### Arguments

Arguments arrive via `$ARGUMENTS` (full string), `$ARGUMENTS[0]` / shorthand `$0` (0-based positional), or named `$issue` when declared in `arguments:`. Indexed args use shell-style quoting ŌĆö `/my-skill "hello world" second` makes `$0` = `hello world`. If you pass args but the body has no placeholder, Claude Code appends `ARGUMENTS: <value>` so the model still sees them. Other useful substitutions: `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}` (resolves bundled-script paths regardless of cwd), `${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}`, `${CLAUDE_EFFORT}`.

### Pitfall: malformed frontmatter

If the YAML is malformed, Claude Code loads the body with **empty metadata** ŌĆö `/name` still works, but there's no `description` to match, so model invocation silently never fires. Run `claude --debug` to surface the parse error.

stage-3.heading: Progressive disclosure: why a lean skill stays cheap
stage-3.body:
**Progressive disclosure** is the mechanism that lets you have dozens of skills without drowning context. It works in three tiers (code.claude.com/docs/en/skills, docs.claude.com agent-skills overview):

1. **Discovery** ŌĆö only `name` + `description` are in context for every skill. This is what the model matches against.
2. **Activation** ŌĆö when a skill is selected, the full `SKILL.md` content enters the conversation as a single message and *stays for the rest of the session*. Claude Code does **not** re-read the file on later turns.
3. **Bundled references** ŌĆö files like `reference.md` load only when Claude actually opens them, because you pointed at them from `SKILL.md`.

### The two design consequences

**Keep the body lean.** Once activated, the body is a recurring per-turn token cost ŌĆö it just sits in context. The docs cap the recommendation at **500 lines** for `SKILL.md` and tell you to push detail into separate files:

```markdown
## Additional resources
- For the full API surface, see [reference.md](reference.md)
- For worked examples, see [examples.md](examples.md)
```

Claude loads `reference.md` only if the task needs it. A 4,000-line API spec next to a 60-line `SKILL.md` costs nothing until the moment it's relevant. (One subtlety: after auto-compaction, Claude Code re-attaches only the most recent invocation of each skill, keeping the first 5,000 tokens, under a shared 25,000-token budget ŌĆö so a huge body can be silently truncated post-compaction. Re-invoke to restore it.)

**Mind the listing budget.** All skill *names* are always listed, but descriptions share a budget that scales at **~1% of the model's context window**. Raise it with the `skillListingBudgetFraction` setting (e.g. `0.02` = 2%) or the `SLASH_COMMAND_TOOL_CHAR_BUDGET` env var (fixed char count). When it overflows, the descriptions of the *least-used* skills get dropped first ŌĆö silently stripping the keywords Claude needs to match them. Run `/doctor` to see what's being shortened or dropped. The lesson: front-load the trigger phrase in every `description`.

### Gotcha: triggering vs. working

Seeing a skill fire only tells you the description matched ŌĆö not that the body did its job. If a skill seems to stop influencing behavior, the content is usually still present and the model is just choosing other tools; strengthen the `description`/body, or escalate to a hook for true enforcement. The docs prescribe a **baseline comparison**: run the same realistic prompts in a fresh session with the skill enabled and disabled, and compare. The official `skill-creator` plugin automates this A/B loop with per-case sub-agent isolation, writing pass-rate vs. token-overhead to `benchmark.json`.

stage-3.heading: Portable skills: author once, mirror into each tool
stage-3.body:
Your team runs both Claude Code and Codex in VS Code. The good news: Agent Skills use the **open `SKILL.md` standard** that both tools build on (agentskills.io, developers.openai.com/codex/skills, code.claude.com/docs/en/skills). The catch: **each tool reads only its own skills directory.** There is no shared resolver.

| Tool | Skills directory (project) | Skills directory (user) | Invocation |
|------|---------------------------|------------------------|------------|
| Claude Code | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | `~/.claude/skills/` | `/name` or model-matched on `description` |
| Codex | `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | `~/.agents/skills/` | `/skills`, `$name`, or model-matched on `description` |

Codex's search order is: repo-level `.agents/skills` (current dir walking up to repo root) ŌåÆ `$HOME/.agents/skills` ŌåÆ `/etc/codex/skills` (admin) ŌåÆ built-ins. It uses the same progressive-disclosure model: name+description first (the initial skills list is capped at **~2% of context, or 8,000 characters**), full body on selection. Both tools require `name` + `description` frontmatter; same-named skills are *not* merged across scopes ŌĆö both can appear in selectors.

### Portability = author once, mirror into each dir

The core `SKILL.md` (frontmatter `name` + `description` + body) is identical across both tools. A **portable skill** is one written in that common subset and made discoverable in each tool's directory. The robust pattern: keep a single canonical source and project it.

```text
~/.agents/skills/             # canonical source of truth
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ summarize-changes/
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ SKILL.md

# Project it into each tool's discovery path (symlink or copy):
ln -s ~/.agents/skills/summarize-changes .claude/skills/summarize-changes
ln -s ~/.agents/skills/summarize-changes .agents/skills/summarize-changes
```

### Gotchas

- **Claude-only frontmatter doesn't port.** `context: fork`, `disable-model-invocation`, `allowed-tools`, `disallowed-tools`, `argument-hint`, `paths` are Claude Code extensions to the standard. Codex ignores them. Keep portable skills to the common subset, or accept tool-specific behavior.
- **A new top-level skills directory needs a restart.** Claude Code live-watches *existing* skill dirs (`~/.claude/skills/`, project `.claude/skills/`) for edits, but creating a *new* top-level dir mid-session requires restarting so it can be watched.
- **Drift is invisible until it bites.** If you copy rather than symlink, the two dirs silently diverge. Gate it in CI ŌĆö see the last section.

stage-3.heading: Sub-agents: a bounded mission in its own context
stage-3.body:
A **sub-agent** *"runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions,"* and *"returns only the summary to the main context"* (code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents). That last clause is the point: the sub-agent can read 40 files, run 12 greps, and burn 30k tokens ŌĆö and your main conversation gets back three paragraphs, not the transcript.

Use one when a side task would flood your main window with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again. Define a *reusable* one when you keep spawning the same kind of worker with the same instructions. (Claude Code also ships built-ins: `Explore` ŌĆö Haiku, read-only, for codebase search; `Plan` ŌĆö read-only research; `general-purpose` ŌĆö all tools. `Explore`/`Plan` skip your CLAUDE.md and git status to stay cheap.)

### File format

Markdown with YAML frontmatter, in `.claude/agents/` (project) or `~/.claude/agents/` (user). Only `name` and `description` are required:

```yaml
---
name: safe-researcher
description: Research agent with restricted, read-only capabilities. Use for codebase exploration that should not write files.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: haiku
---

You are a read-only research agent. Find relevant files, read them,
and return a summary with specific file:line references. Never edit.
```

The **markdown body becomes the sub-agent's entire system prompt** ŌĆö it does not inherit the main Claude Code system prompt, only basic environment details.

### The three levers

| Lever | Field | Why it matters |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| **Least-privilege tools** | `tools:` (allowlist) or `disallowedTools:` (denylist) | Omit `tools` and the sub-agent inherits *all* tools. Set `tools: Read, Grep, Glob` to lock it to exactly the mission ŌĆö never `Write` or `Edit`. Or invert with `disallowedTools: Write, Edit` to keep everything else. This is enforcement, not a suggestion. |
| **Cheaper model** | `model:` | `sonnet` / `opus` / `haiku` / `fable` / a full ID / `inherit` (default). Route bounded grunt work to `haiku`; reserve the strong model for the main thread. |
| **Context isolation** | (inherent) | The sub-agent's reads never enter your main window. Only its return summary does. |

### Two ways skills and sub-agents compose

- **Skill with `context: fork`** ŌĆö the skill *body* becomes the task, and `agent: Explore` (or a custom agent) provides the execution environment. The forked context sees only the SKILL.md content, not your conversation history. If omitted, `agent` defaults to `general-purpose`.
- **Sub-agent with a `skills:` field** ŌĆö a custom sub-agent that *preloads* skills as reference material at startup. Per the docs, the **full skill content is injected, not just the description** (the inverse of a normal session).

### Pitfalls

- **Don't fork a skill that has no task.** `context: fork` on a pure-guidelines skill ("use these API conventions") gives the sub-agent rules but no actionable prompt ŌĆö it returns nothing useful. (This warning is verbatim in the docs.)
- **The implementer anti-pattern.** Sub-agents shine as *researchers* (read, summarize, return). Handing a sub-agent a large *implementation* task is risky: it can't ask you clarifying questions mid-flight, and its edits land without your review of the reasoning. Prefer: sub-agent gathers context ŌåÆ returns summary ŌåÆ main thread implements. (See the `/design-subagents` skill.)
- **No conversation memory.** Each invocation starts cold. Pass everything it needs in the delegation, or via files on disk.

stage-3.heading: Hooks: enforcement that fires no matter what
Hooks: events, handler types, and the patterns that pay off
stage-3.body:
Most people know `PreToolUse` / `PostToolUse`, but the event surface is far wider (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks):

- **Session**: `SessionStart`, `Setup`, `SessionEnd`, `InstructionsLoaded`
- **Per-turn**: `UserPromptSubmit`, `UserPromptExpansion`, `Stop`, `StopFailure`
- **Tool loop**: `PreToolUse`, `PermissionRequest`, `PermissionDenied`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PostToolBatch`
- **Async/env**: `FileChanged`, `CwdChanged`, `ConfigChange`, `Notification`, `MessageDisplay`, `WorktreeCreate`, `WorktreeRemove`
- **Sub-agent/task**: `SubagentStart`, `SubagentStop`, `TaskCreated`, `TaskCompleted`, `TeammateIdle`
- **MCP**: `Elicitation`, `ElicitationResult`
- **Compaction**: `PreCompact`, `PostCompact`

And handlers are not limited to shell commands. The `type` can be:

| `type` | What it does |
|--------|--------------|
| `command` | Shell command; JSON on stdin, control via exit code / stdout JSON |
| `http` | POST the event JSON to a URL; response drives the decision |
| `mcp_tool` | Call a tool on a configured MCP server |
| `prompt` | Single-turn LLM evaluation for a yes/no decision |
| `agent` | Spawn a sub-agent with tool access (**experimental**) |

That means a lifecycle event can fire an LLM prompt or even a sub-agent, not just a script.

### High-value patterns

- **Format/lint on write** ŌĆö `PostToolUse` matching `Edit|Write` runs `prettier`/`ruff` so the model never sees unformatted code it then has to re-fix.
- **Block dangerous commands** ŌĆö `PreToolUse` matching `Bash` with `if: "Bash(rm -rf *)"` denies destructively.
- **Inject context at prompt time** ŌĆö `UserPromptSubmit` returns `hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext` to prepend live state (current branch, failing tests) to every prompt.
- **Enforce "never end with uncommitted changes"** ŌĆö a `Stop` hook with `{"decision": "block"}` if `git status --short` is non-empty.

### Skill vs. hook ŌĆö the decision

| | Skill | Hook |
|---|-------|------|
| Invocation | Model decides (description match) or `/name` | Deterministic on event+matcher |
| Can it be skipped? | Yes ŌĆö model may not invoke it | No ŌĆö fires regardless |
| Good for | *Procedures* you want applied when relevant | *Policies* that must always hold |
| Failure if misused | Advisory rule silently ignored | Over-blocking that frustrates real work |

Rule of thumb: if the consequence of skipping it is "a bit less convenient," use a skill. If it's "a broken build, a leaked secret, or a force-pushed `main`," use a hook.

### Gotcha

Hooks in skills/agents are scoped to that component's lifecycle, but **plugin sub-agents cannot use the `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter** ŌĆö those fields are silently ignored when an agent loads from a plugin (security). If you need them, copy the agent into `.claude/agents/`.

stage-3.heading: MCP servers: typed gateways to external systems
stage-3.body:
An **MCP server** is an external tool Claude calls over the Model Context Protocol ŌĆö GitHub, Postgres, a tracker. Connect one *"when you find yourself copying data into chat from another tool"* (code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp).

### Transports

| Transport | When | Add command |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| **http** (alias `streamable-http`) | Remote cloud services; supports OAuth | `claude mcp add --transport http notion https://mcp.notion.com/mcp` |
| **stdio** | Local processes, custom scripts, direct system access | `claude mcp add --env KEY=val --transport stdio airtable -- npx -y airtable-mcp-server` |
| **sse** | **Deprecated** ŌĆö use http | `claude mcp add --transport sse ...` |
| **ws** | Persistent bidirectional push; no OAuth, no `--transport` flag (JSON only) | via `claude mcp add-json` |

For stdio, everything after `--` is passed to the server untouched; `--` separates Claude's own flags (`--transport`, `--env`, `--scope`) from the server command. In `.mcp.json`, the `type` field accepts `streamable-http` as an alias for `http` so configs copied from server docs work unchanged.

### Scopes

| Scope (`--scope`) | Stored in | Shared? |
|-------------------|-----------|---------|
| `local` (default) | `~/.claude.json`, keyed to *this project's path* ŌĆö private to you | No |
| `project` | `.mcp.json` in repo root | **Yes ŌĆö check it in** |
| `user` | `~/.claude.json` | Across your projects |

Project scope is the team mechanism. Claude Code prompts each member for approval before using project-scoped servers (a prompt-injection guardrail; they show as `ŌÅĖ Pending approval` in `claude mcp list`); reset with `claude mcp reset-project-choices`.

### .mcp.json with secrets kept out of the file

The critical pattern: **commit the config, never the secret.** Claude Code expands `${VAR}` and `${VAR:-default}` in the `command`, `args`, `env`, `url`, and `headers` fields:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "github": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/",
      "headers": {
        "Authorization": "Bearer ${GITHUB_PAT}"
      }
    },
    "local-db": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres", "${DATABASE_URL}"],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
```

`${GITHUB_PAT}` and `${DATABASE_URL}` resolve from each developer's environment. The repo holds the *shape*; the secret lives in the shell/`.env`. **If a required var is unset with no default, Claude Code fails to parse the config** ŌĆö fail-loud, by design.

### Operational guardrails

- Claude Code warns when an MCP tool returns **>10,000 tokens**; raise with `MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS` (e.g. `=50000`). This warning is your early signal that a server is a context hog.
- Per-server `timeout` (ms) in the `.mcp.json` entry caps each tool call (e.g. `"timeout": 600000`); values below 1000 fall through to `MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT`. Startup timeout is `MCP_TIMEOUT`.
- `/mcp` shows status and tool counts; `claude mcp list` / `get <name>` from the CLI.

stage-3.heading: The MCP context-cost problem (contested)
stage-3.body:
MCP is the primitive most likely to quietly wreck your setup, because **loading many MCP tools consumes a large share of the context window before work begins.** Each always-on server pays its tool-schema tax every single session. This is a genuine open debate, and your team should pick a side deliberately.

### The two camps

**Thin-gateway / CLI-first** (blog.sshh.io): an MCP server should be a thin, secure gateway exposing a *few high-level* tools. Migrate stateless integrations ŌĆö Jira, GitHub, AWS ŌĆö to plain CLIs that Claude calls via `Bash`, and reserve MCP for genuinely *stateful* clients like Playwright (a live browser session). A CLI call costs tokens only when invoked; an always-on MCP server pays up front.

**Compress-don't-restrict** (mksg.lu/blog/context-mode): keep the tool richness, but route MCP outputs through a compression middleware for roughly **98% context reduction**. Don't amputate capability; shrink the payload.

| | Thin-gateway / CLI-first | Compress-don't-restrict |
|---|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| Core move | Fewer tools; CLIs for stateless services | Keep tools; compress outputs |
| Best when | Stateless integrations (GitHub, Jira, AWS) | You genuinely need many tools |
| Reserve MCP for | Stateful clients (Playwright, live DB sessions) | ŌĆö |
| Risk | Lose discoverability of niche tools | Middleware complexity; compression can drop signal |

This ecosystem's own tool-interface policy (the project ADR on CLI-first / MCP-adapters) lands on the thin-gateway side: *core library first, CLI by default, Skill for procedure, MCP only for a justified integration boundary.* A defensible default ŌĆö but the contest is live; note it, don't pretend it's settled.

### Mitigations available today

- **Tool search** defers MCP tool schemas behind a `ToolSearch` call instead of loading all of them up front ŌĆö directly attacks the up-front cost. Control with the `ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH` env var.
- **`alwaysLoad`** per-server forces a server's tools to load eagerly when you truly need them present at first prompt.
- **Per-tool overrides** like `anthropic/maxResultSizeChars` let a single chatty tool declare its own output cap independent of `MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS`.
- **Approval gating / `disabledMcpjsonServers`** lets a team ship a rich `.mcp.json` while individuals opt into only what they use.

stage-3.heading: When to use what: the decision table
stage-3.body:
The four primitives are not interchangeable. Misclassifying a need is the most common and most expensive mistake in this stage. Here is the full decision surface:

| You wantŌĆ” | Reach for | Because |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| A fact / convention always in context | `CLAUDE.md` (or `user-invocable: false` skill) | Cheap passive knowledge; no procedure |
| A *procedure* applied when relevant | **Skill** | Model-invoked on description; body loads on demand |
| A workflow *you* trigger with side effects | **Skill** + `disable-model-invocation: true` | You control timing (`/deploy`) |
| A rule that must hold 100% of the time | **Hook** | Deterministic, enforced, can hard-block |
| Format/lint/validate after every edit | **Hook** (`PostToolUse`) | Fires regardless of model choice |
| A bounded sub-task without context pollution | **Sub-agent** | Own window, returns only a summary |
| The same worker spawned repeatedly | **Reusable sub-agent** in `.claude/agents/` | Encodes system prompt + tool restrictions once |
| Read/act on an external system | **MCP server** | Typed protocol gateway |
| A *stateless* external call (GitHub, Jira) | **CLI via Bash** (often, not MCP) | Avoids always-on tool-schema tax |
| Bundle several of the above to ship to a team | **Plugin + marketplace** | One install delivers skills+agents+hooks+MCP |

### Worked disambiguations

- *"Always run our linter"* ŌåÆ hook, not skill. A skill can be skipped; a `PostToolUse` hook cannot.
- *"Summarize a PR"* ŌåÆ skill (procedure), optionally `context: fork` to keep the diff out of your main window.
- *"Never let the agent push to main"* ŌåÆ `PreToolUse` hook with `deny`. Putting it in `CLAUDE.md` is the classic mistake ŌĆö it's advisory and will eventually be ignored.
- *"Query our prod database"* ŌåÆ MCP (stateful connection) or a read-only CLI wrapper, depending on which camp above.
- *"Explore an unfamiliar module"* ŌåÆ sub-agent (read-heavy, returns a map, spares your context).

stage-3.heading: The contested case: passive docs vs. model-invoked skills
stage-3.body:
Before you skill-ify everything, sit with the strongest counter-evidence ŌĆö because it changes *what you build first*.

### The finding

In Vercel's published agent evals, an **`AGENTS.md` docs-index scored 100% while Skills scored 53ŌĆō79%**, and *the skill was never invoked in 56% of cases* (vercel.com/blog/agents-md-outperforms-skills-in-our-agent-evals). For supplying **always-true knowledge**, a static docs-index currently outperforms model-invoked skills.

### The two readings

- **passive-context-wins-now**: results matter today. If knowledge should *always* be available, put it in passive context (`AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md`), where it can't fail to trigger. The 53ŌĆō79% reflects a real, current cost: a skill that doesn't fire does nothing.
- **skills-for-actions**: skills win for *vertical, explicitly-triggered action workflows* ŌĆö the place where you *want* on-demand loading, not always-on knowledge. The invocation gap is a current tool-use limitation that will close.

Both can be right because they're about different jobs. The synthesis your team should adopt:

| Need | Put it in | Rationale |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| Always-true knowledge (conventions, architecture facts) | Passive context (`CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md`) | Can't fail to trigger; eval-backed |
| Always-true *enforcement* | Hook | Deterministic |
| On-demand *procedure / action* | Skill | This is exactly the skill sweet spot |

The practical implication: **don't convert reference knowledge into a skill just because skills are new.** The very first `code.claude.com/docs/en/skills` guidance agrees ŌĆö *a fact belongs in CLAUDE.md, a procedure belongs in a skill.* Skill-ify procedures; keep facts passive.

stage-3.heading: Packaging: plugins and marketplaces for the whole team
stage-3.body:
Skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers are individually useful, but a team wants to *ship a bundle and have everyone get it with one command.* That's a **plugin**, distributed through a **marketplace**.

### Plugin layout

```text
my-plugin/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ plugin.json        # ONLY the manifest lives here
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/                # all components at the plugin ROOT
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ pr-summary/SKILL.md
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ safe-researcher.md
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ hooks.json
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .mcp.json              # bundled MCP servers
```

A critical and easy-to-miss rule: **only `plugin.json` goes inside `.claude-plugin/`.** Every component directory (`skills/`, `agents/`, `hooks/`, `commands/`) must be at the plugin *root*. Putting them inside `.claude-plugin/` is the #1 "skills not appearing" bug. Remember too that plugin components are **namespaced** ŌĆö an agent `reviewer` in plugin `team-infra` appears as `team-infra:reviewer`, and plugin sub-agents silently drop `hooks`/`mcpServers`/`permissionMode` frontmatter (security).

### plugin.json

```json
{
  "name": "team-infra",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "description": "Shared skills, hooks, and MCP for the platform team",
  "author": { "name": "Platform Team", "email": "infra@example.com" }
}
```

`name` is the only required field; omit the manifest entirely and components auto-discover with the name derived from the directory.

### marketplace.json

In `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` at the catalog repo root:

```json
{
  "name": "company-tools",
  "owner": { "name": "DevTools Team", "email": "devtools@example.com" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "team-infra",
      "source": "./plugins/team-infra",
      "description": "Shared platform skills, hooks, and MCP",
      "version": "1.2.0"
    },
    {
      "name": "deployment-tools",
      "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "company/deploy-plugin" }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Install flow

```text
/plugin marketplace add company/tools-marketplace   # or a local path
/plugin install team-infra@company-tools
/reload-plugins                                      # activate in current session
```

This is how *"describe it again"* becomes *"it's built in"* for a whole team: one marketplace entry delivers the skills, the enforcement hooks, the restricted sub-agents, and the MCP wiring in a single versioned install. Pin `version` so members only update when you bump it.

stage-3.heading: Configure once, run in both Claude Code and Codex
stage-3.body:
The lead's real goal is *configure once, get identical rules whether a dev opens Claude Code or Codex in VS Code.* Nothing makes this automatic ŌĆö both tools read their *own* directories. The plan (project-meta `render_agents_md.py` + `check_agent_surface_sync.py`) is to **keep one canonical config and project it**, with a CI drift check.

### The projection

1. **Instructions.** `CLAUDE.md` is canonical; `AGENTS.md` is the Codex-facing mirror (agents.md, developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md). Generate or symlink `AGENTS.md` from `CLAUDE.md` rather than maintaining two copies ŌĆö in this ecosystem via `render_agents_md.py`.
2. **Skills.** Keep a portable source (e.g. `~/.agents/skills/`) and mirror it into each tool's discovery dir ŌĆö `.claude/skills/` for Claude, `.agents/skills/` for Codex ŌĆö by symlink where possible.
3. **Drift check in CI.** `check_agent_surface_sync.py` is read-only: it verifies the root instruction files resolve to the same content and flags mirror drift between the skill directories (it also enforces the 500-line `SKILL.md` body limit). Wire it so CI *fails* on divergence ŌĆö that turns "the two tools quietly disagree" from a silent bug into a blocked merge.

### Layout

```text
repo/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ CLAUDE.md                    # canonical instructions
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ AGENTS.md                    # generated/symlinked from CLAUDE.md (Codex)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude/
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/  -> mirror of portable source
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/  *.md             # sub-agents (Claude-specific format)
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/                    # hook scripts
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ settings.json             # hooks + permissions (shareable)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .agents/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ skills/  -> mirror of portable source
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .mcp.json                    # project MCP servers (secrets via ${VAR})
```

### What ports cleanly vs. what doesn't

| Layer | Portable across both tools? |
|-------|-----------------------------|
| Instructions | Yes ŌĆö `CLAUDE.md` Ōćä `AGENTS.md` projection |
| Skill core (`name`/`description`/body) | Yes ŌĆö open `SKILL.md` standard |
| Claude skill extensions (`context: fork`, `allowed-tools`, `paths`) | No ŌĆö Claude-only |
| Hooks / `settings.json` | No ŌĆö Claude Code mechanism |
| Sub-agent files | No ŌĆö Claude Code format |
| `.mcp.json` MCP servers | Partially ŌĆö both speak MCP, but config files and scoping differ |

The honest takeaway: **instructions and skill bodies project cleanly; enforcement (hooks) and orchestration (sub-agents) are currently Claude-Code-specific.** Standardize what's standardizable, gate it with the drift check, and keep the tool-specific enforcement layer in each tool's native config ŌĆö don't fake a portability that the formats don't yet support.

stage-3.heading: Common pitfalls and failure modes
stage-3.body:
A consolidated list of the traps, since each primitive fails differently.

### Skills
- **Description doesn't trigger.** Missing the keywords users actually say. Fix: front-load the trigger phrase; verify with `What skills are available?`; check `/doctor` for dropped descriptions.
- **Triggers but doesn't work.** The body loaded; the model chose other tools. Strengthen the body, or escalate to a hook for true enforcement. Validate with a fresh-session baseline A/B, not a same-session spot check.
- **Bloated body.** Over 500 lines and full of narration. It's a recurring per-turn cost (and can be truncated to 5,000 tokens after compaction). Move detail to `reference.md` (progressive disclosure).
- **Malformed frontmatter.** Claude loads the body with *empty metadata* ŌĆö `/name` still works but there's no description to match. Run `claude --debug` to see the parse error.

### Hooks
- **Used `exit 1` to block.** Only `exit 2` (or a JSON `deny`/`block`) blocks; `1` is non-blocking. Silent failure to enforce.
- **Policy written in `CLAUDE.md` instead of a hook.** The category error of this whole stage. Advisory ŌēĀ enforced.
- **Over-broad matcher.** `"*"` on `PreToolUse` running a slow script taxes every tool call. Scope with `matcher` + `if`.
- **Plugin sub-agent hooks ignored.** `hooks`/`mcpServers`/`permissionMode` are dropped from plugin-loaded agents (security). Copy into `.claude/agents/` if needed.

### Sub-agents
- **Forking a task-less skill.** `context: fork` with only guidelines ŌåÆ no actionable prompt ŌåÆ empty return.
- **Using a sub-agent as an implementer.** It can't ask clarifying questions and its edits skip your review. Prefer researcher ŌåÆ summary ŌåÆ main-thread implements.
- **Forgetting it has no memory.** Each call starts cold; pass context in the delegation or via files.
- **Forgot `tools:` entirely.** Omitting it inherits *all* tools ŌĆö the opposite of least-privilege. Set `tools:` or `disallowedTools:`.

### MCP
- **Secret committed in `.mcp.json`.** Use `${VAR}`/`${VAR:-default}` expansion (works in `command`/`args`/`env`/`url`/`headers`); keep the token in the environment. The repo holds shape, never secrets.
- **Context blown before work starts.** Too many always-on tools. Lean on tool-search, prune to a thin gateway, or compress outputs ŌĆö and treat the >10k-token MCP-output warning as your signal.
- **Forgot project-scope approval.** Teammates must approve project-scoped servers; `claude mcp reset-project-choices` to redo.

### Portability / cross-tool
- **Copied skills drift.** Symlink from one canonical source, and fail CI on divergence with the surface-sync check.
- **Assumed hooks/agents port to Codex.** They don't. Only instructions and skill cores project; keep enforcement in each tool's native config.

stage-4.heading: The shelf nobody built
stage-4.body:
Three people on your team have written a code-review skill. One lives in Maya's `~/.claude/skills/review/`, one is copy-pasted into the `.claude/commands/` of the payments repo, and the third is a half-remembered prompt Devon keeps in a Notion doc. They are 80% the same and 20% subtly divergent ŌĆö different severity ladders, different ideas about what "done" means. The waste was never the writing. Writing a good review skill once is cheap. The waste is that there is **no shelf everyone pulls from**, so the skill gets re-derived, drifts, and rots in three places at once.

This stage is about building that shelf. Claude Code's answer has two parts: a **plugin** (the unit you put *on* the shelf ŌĆö a versioned bundle of skills, sub-agents, hooks, MCP servers, and LSP servers) and a **plugin marketplace** (the shelf itself ŌĆö a catalog repo the team adds once and pulls updates from). By the end you will be able to take that scattered review skill, package it as a plugin, publish it in a private team marketplace, and have everyone install it with two commands ŌĆö plus understand exactly what you are trusting when you do, because installing a plugin is one consent click over a lot of executable surface.

This is depth, not a tour. Every format below is copy-able and checked against the official docs at `code.claude.com/docs` (`/plugins`, `/plugin-marketplaces`, `/discover-plugins`, `/plugins-reference`).

stage-4.heading: What a plugin actually is
stage-4.body:
A **plugin** is a versioned bundle of extensions ŌĆö slash commands, sub-agents, skills, hooks, MCP servers, and LSP servers ŌĆö packaged into one installable unit (source: `code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins`). It is the difference between "here's a file, copy it into your `.claude/`" and "install `code-review@team-tools` and you have the same thing I have, with updates."

The docs frame the standalone-vs-plugin choice as a tradeoff over skill *names* and *sharing*: standalone `.claude/` skills get short names (`/review`); plugin skills are always namespaced (`/plugin-name:review`). Use standalone for personal/project-only iteration; reach for a plugin when you want versioned releases reusable across projects and teammates.

### The minimum: a manifest

Every plugin is a directory. The defining file is the manifest at `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "code-review",
  "description": "Team code-review skill: severity ladder, security pass, test-coverage check",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "author": { "name": "DevTools Team", "email": "devtools@acme.com" }
}
```

Fields that matter (from the official manifest table):

| Field | Required | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | yes | Unique id **and** skill namespace. Skills become `/code-review:<skill>`. kebab-case, no spaces. |
| `description` | recommended | Shown in the plugin manager when teammates browse. |
| `version` | optional | If set, users only get updates when you **bump** it. If omitted on a git-hosted plugin, the commit SHA is used and every commit counts as a new version. |
| `author` | optional | Attribution (`name` required, `email` optional). |

The manifest also accepts `homepage`, `repository`, `license`, and `keywords` (see the full manifest schema in `/plugins-reference`). The namespacing is deliberate: a plugin skill is *always* invoked as `/plugin-name:skill-name` (e.g. `/code-review:review`) so two plugins can both ship a `review` skill without colliding. You change the prefix by changing `name`.

Note a subtlety from the docs: the manifest is technically *optional* if every component sits in its default location ŌĆö a directory with just `skills/` can load. But for anything you intend to version and share, write the `plugin.json`; it is what the plugin manager displays and what drives version-based updates.

stage-4.heading: Plugin directory layout ŌĆö and the one mistake everyone makes
stage-4.body:
Components live in fixed directories at the **plugin root**, not inside `.claude-plugin/`. This is the single most common authoring error, and the docs flag it explicitly with a Warning.

```text
code-review/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ plugin.json          # ONLY this goes in .claude-plugin/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ review/
Ōöé       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ SKILL.md         # ŌåÆ /code-review:review
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ security-reviewer.md  # a sub-agent
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ hooks.json            # event handlers
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ monitors/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ monitors.json         # background watchers (tail logs, files, status)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .mcp.json                 # MCP server configs
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .lsp.json                 # language-server configs
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ commands/                 # legacy: flat .md skills (use skills/ for new work)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ bin/                      # executables added to PATH while enabled
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ settings.json             # default settings when the plugin is enabled
```

| Directory / file | Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` | inside `.claude-plugin/` | the manifest ŌĆö **the only thing** in here |
| `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | root | model-invoked skills |
| `commands/*.md` | root | legacy flat-Markdown skills; docs say use `skills/` for new plugins |
| `agents/*.md` | root | sub-agent definitions |
| `hooks/hooks.json` | root | event handlers (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, ŌĆ”) |
| `monitors/monitors.json` | root | background monitors; each `command`'s stdout lines become notifications |
| `.mcp.json` | root | MCP server configs |
| `.lsp.json` | root | LSP servers for code intelligence |
| `bin/` | root | executables added to the Bash tool's `$PATH` while the plugin is enabled |
| `settings.json` | root | default settings (only `agent` and `subagentStatusLine` honored; unknown keys silently ignored) |

From the docs (verbatim Warning): *"Don't put `commands/`, `agents/`, `skills/`, or `hooks/` inside the `.claude-plugin/` directory. Only `plugin.json` goes inside `.claude-plugin/`. All other directories must be at the plugin root level."* If your skills don't show up after install, this is the first thing to check.

A plugin shipping exactly one skill may put `SKILL.md` at the plugin root and skip the `skills/` directory entirely (Claude Code uses the frontmatter `name` for the invocation); use the `skills/` layout once you expect more than one.

stage-4.heading: The components: what you can bundle
stage-4.body:
### Skills

A skill is a folder under `skills/` containing a `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter. The `description` is load-bearing ŌĆö it's how Claude decides when to auto-invoke:

```markdown
---
description: Reviews code for bugs, security, and performance. Use when reviewing code, checking PRs, or analyzing code quality.
---

When reviewing code, check for:
1. Code organization and structure
2. Error handling and edge cases
3. Security concerns (injection, secrets, authz)
4. Test coverage

Report findings as severity-ranked, actionable items. Be concise.
```

Add `disable-model-invocation: true` to the frontmatter (the docs use exactly this in their quickstart) if you want the skill to be *only* explicitly invokable (`/code-review:review`) and never auto-fired by the model. `$ARGUMENTS` in the body captures text the user types after the skill name (e.g. `/code-review:review src/auth.ts`).

### Sub-agents

Drop agent definitions in `agents/*.md`. A `settings.json` at the plugin root with `{"agent": "security-reviewer"}` can even make one of the plugin's agents the **main thread** when the plugin is enabled ŌĆö applying its system prompt, tool restrictions, and model. That's a plugin that changes how Claude Code behaves by default. (Only `agent` and `subagentStatusLine` are honored in plugin `settings.json`; other keys are silently ignored.)

### Hooks

Hooks live in `hooks/hooks.json` and use the exact same format as the `hooks` block in `settings.json` ŌĆö so migration is copy-paste. The command receives hook input as JSON on stdin; the docs use `jq` to pull the file path out:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "hooks": [
          { "type": "command", "command": "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/lint.sh" }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` resolves to the plugin's installed (cached) location ŌĆö use it for any path into the plugin, because plugins are **copied to a cache**, so `../shared-utils` and absolute author-machine paths will not resolve. For state that must *survive plugin updates* (a SQLite db, a token cache), use `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` instead ŌĆö the cache directory is replaced on update, the data directory is not.

### MCP servers

Bundle an MCP server config in `.mcp.json` (or inline `mcpServers` in the marketplace entry). This is how the official `github`, `linear`, `sentry`, `slack`, `notion`, `figma`, `vercel`, and `supabase` plugins ship pre-configured MCP integrations ŌĆö install the plugin, get the server, no manual `.mcp.json` editing.

### LSP servers

`.lsp.json` wires a language server for real-time diagnostics and code navigation. The official marketplace already ships `pyright-lsp`, `gopls-lsp`, `typescript-lsp`, `rust-analyzer-lsp`, and more; author your own only for languages not covered. The user must have the language-server **binary** on `$PATH` ŌĆö if it's missing you'll see `Executable not found in $PATH` in the `/plugin` Errors tab. Minimal `.lsp.json`:

```json
{ "go": { "command": "gopls", "args": ["serve"], "extensionToLanguage": { ".go": "go" } } }
```

### Background monitors

`monitors/monitors.json` lets a plugin watch a log, file, or external status in the background; each stdout line from the monitor's `command` is delivered to Claude as a notification during the session ŌĆö no instruction to "start watching" needed.

stage-4.heading: Develop and test before you publish
stage-4.body:
You do not need a marketplace to iterate. Load a plugin straight off disk:

```bash
claude --plugin-dir ./code-review
```

Then inside the session, after edits, pick up changes without restarting:

```text
/reload-plugins
```

`/reload-plugins` reloads plugins, skills, agents, hooks, and plugin MCP/LSP servers, and reports counts for each. Verify the three surfaces individually: run `/code-review:review`, confirm agents appear in `/agents`, and confirm hooks fire on a real edit.

Load several at once by repeating the flag: `claude --plugin-dir ./a --plugin-dir ./b`. When a `--plugin-dir` plugin shares a name with an installed marketplace plugin, the local copy wins for that session ŌĆö so you can test a patch to something already installed without uninstalling it (exception: plugins force-enabled/disabled by managed settings can't be overridden this way). `--plugin-dir` also accepts a `.zip` archive (Claude Code v2.1.128+), and `--plugin-url https://ŌĆ”/plugin.zip` fetches and loads an archive for that session only.

Validate the structure before you ship:

```bash
claude plugin validate ./code-review     # or /plugin validate ./code-review in-session
```

Pointed at a **plugin** directory, the validator checks `plugin.json` plus the frontmatter of every skill, agent, and command, and the JSON of `hooks/hooks.json`. Pointed at a **marketplace** directory, it checks `marketplace.json` only (schema, duplicate plugin names, `..` path traversal, version mismatches against each `plugin.json`). A malformed `hooks.json` prevents the **entire plugin** from loading, so this catches a real failure mode.

### Converting your scattered skill

Migrating the existing `.claude/` review skill is mechanical (these are the docs' own migration steps):

```bash
mkdir -p code-review/.claude-plugin
# write plugin.json (above), then:
cp -r .claude/skills/review code-review/skills/
```

Hooks from a `settings.json` move verbatim into `hooks/hooks.json` (same format). Afterward, **delete the originals** from `.claude/` ŌĆö project/user `.claude/agents/` definitions override same-named plugin agents, so leaving them creates silent shadowing.

If you'd rather skip `--plugin-dir` on every launch while iterating, `claude plugin init my-tool` scaffolds a plugin under `~/.claude/skills/my-tool/` that auto-loads next session as `my-tool@skills-dir`.

stage-4.heading: What a marketplace is
stage-4.body:
A **plugin marketplace** is a catalog repo defined by a single file, `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`, that lists plugins and where to fetch each one. Teams **add it once**, install the plugins they need, and pull updates by refreshing the catalog (sources: `code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces`, `/discover-plugins`).

The mental model from the docs: adding a marketplace is *"like adding an app store"* ŌĆö it gives you the catalog to browse, but you still choose which plugins to install individually. Adding the marketplace installs **nothing**.

### The catalog file

```json
{
  "name": "acme-tools",
  "owner": { "name": "DevTools Team", "email": "devtools@acme.com" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "code-review",
      "source": "./plugins/code-review",
      "description": "Team code-review skill",
      "version": "1.2.0"
    },
    {
      "name": "deploy-tools",
      "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "acme/deploy-plugin" },
      "description": "Deployment automation"
    }
  ]
}
```

Required top-level fields are `name` (kebab-case, public-facing ŌĆö it's the `@acme-tools` in `/plugin install x@acme-tools`), `owner` (object, `name` required, `email` optional), and `plugins` (array). Each plugin entry needs at minimum `name` and `source`. Useful optionals: `metadata.pluginRoot: "./plugins"` lets you write `"source": "code-review"` instead of `"./plugins/code-review"`; a plugin entry may also carry `displayName` (a spaced/cased human label, v2.1.143+) and `defaultEnabled: false` (install disabled until the user opts in, v2.1.154+).

Two constraints worth knowing up front: each user can register only **one marketplace per name** ŌĆö adding a second with the same `name` replaces the first, so list all your plugins in one `marketplace.json`. And a set of names (`claude-plugins-official`, `claude-community`, `anthropic-*`, etc.) is **reserved** for Anthropic and rejected for third-party marketplaces, along with names that impersonate official ones.

stage-4.heading: Plugin sources: where each plugin is fetched from
stage-4.body:
The `source` field on a plugin entry is the most important authoring decision in a marketplace, because it determines pinning, bandwidth, and whether relative paths even work.

| Source | Form | Fields | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative path | `"./plugins/x"` | ŌĆö | Must start with `./`; resolved from marketplace **root** (the dir containing `.claude-plugin/`), never `..`. Only works when the marketplace is added via git, not via a bare URL to `marketplace.json`. |
| `github` | object | `repo`, `ref?`, `sha?` | `owner/repo` shorthand. |
| `url` | object | `url`, `ref?`, `sha?` | Any git URL (GitLab, Bitbucket, self-hosted); `.git` suffix optional. |
| `git-subdir` | object | `url`, `path`, `ref?`, `sha?` | Sparse partial clone ŌĆö for a plugin inside a monorepo. `url` also takes `owner/repo` or SSH form. |
| `npm` | object | `package`, `version?`, `registry?` | Installed via `npm install`; supports private registries. |

Pinning, for the three git-based sources (`github`, `url`, `git-subdir`):

```json
{
  "name": "code-review",
  "source": {
    "source": "github",
    "repo": "acme/code-review",
    "ref": "v1.2.0",
    "sha": "a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0"
  }
}
```

When both `ref` and `sha` are set, **`sha` is the effective pin** ŌĆö Claude Code checks out that exact commit (a full 40-character SHA), and on GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket the install still succeeds even if the branch named by `ref` was later deleted, as long as the commit is reachable. (Exception: servers like AWS CodeCommit can't fetch by SHA, so there the `ref` must still exist.) For a security-reviewed internal marketplace, pinning `sha` is how you guarantee everyone runs the exact bytes you audited.

**Marketplace source vs plugin source** are distinct and pinned independently: the marketplace source (passed to `/plugin marketplace add`) is where the *catalog* lives and supports `ref` but **not** `sha`; each plugin's `source` is where that *plugin* lives and supports both. A catalog at `acme/plugin-catalog` can list a plugin fetched from `acme/code-review` ŌĆö different repos.

stage-4.heading: The full lifecycle: add, install, update, remove
stage-4.body:
Every `/plugin` command has a non-interactive `claude plugin ŌĆ”` twin for scripting and CI. Shortcuts: `/plugin market` for `marketplace`, `rm` for `remove`.

### Add the marketplace (once per teammate)

```text
/plugin marketplace add acme/claude-plugins          # GitHub owner/repo
/plugin marketplace add acme/claude-plugins@v2.0     # pin a branch/tag (GitHub shorthand: @ref)
/plugin marketplace add https://gitlab.com/acme/plugins.git
/plugin marketplace add https://gitlab.com/acme/plugins.git#v1.0.0   # git URL: #ref
/plugin marketplace add git@gitlab.com:acme/plugins.git             # SSH
/plugin marketplace add ./my-marketplace             # local, for testing
```

### Install plugins (each person picks what they need)

```text
/plugin install code-review@acme-tools     # installs to USER scope by default
```

To choose a different scope from the CLI you can pass `--scope project|local|user`; from the UI, press Enter on the plugin and pick a scope.

### Update ŌĆö the whole point of the shelf

```text
/plugin marketplace update acme-tools   # refresh the catalog from its source
```

When you ship `code-review` v1.3.0, you push to the marketplace repo; teammates run `marketplace update` (or get it via auto-update at startup) and `/reload-plugins`. No more drift across three copies.

### Manage installed plugins

```text
/plugin list                              # add --enabled / --disabled
/plugin disable code-review@acme-tools    # turn off without uninstalling
/plugin enable  code-review@acme-tools
/plugin uninstall code-review@acme-tools
/reload-plugins                           # apply changes mid-session
```

`/plugin` with no args opens a four-tab UI you cycle with **Tab**: **Discover**, **Installed**, **Marketplaces**, **Errors**. In the Discover detail pane, recent versions show a **Context cost** estimate (tokens added per turn, v2.1.143+), a **Last updated** date (v2.1.144+), and a **Will install** list enumerating every command, agent, skill, hook, and MCP/LSP server (v2.1.145+) ŌĆö review this before you install. The same component inventory is available non-interactively via `claude plugin details`.

### Remove

```text
/plugin marketplace remove acme-tools
```

**Gotcha (verbatim Warning):** *"Removing a marketplace will uninstall any plugins you installed from it."* (Precisely: removing it from its *last remaining scope* uninstalls them.) To merely refresh, use `marketplace update`, never `remove` + re-add.

stage-4.heading: Making it automatic for the team
Version management ŌĆö the silent-no-op trap
stage-4.body:
Versions decide cache paths and whether `/plugin update` does anything. If the resolved version matches what a user already has, both `/plugin update` and auto-update skip the plugin. Claude Code resolves a plugin's version from the first of these that's set:

1. `version` in the plugin's `plugin.json`
2. `version` in the marketplace entry
3. the git commit SHA of the plugin's source

Two strategies, and you must pick one consciously:

| Strategy | How | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Explicit version** | set `version` in `plugin.json`, bump every release | Users update only when the string changes. Predictable; requires discipline. |
| **SHA-as-version** | omit `version` on a git-hosted plugin (`github`, `url`, `git-subdir`, or a relative path in a git-hosted marketplace) | Every commit is a new version. Zero ceremony; good for internal, fast-moving plugins. |

The trap, straight from the docs Warning: if `plugin.json` says `"version": "1.0.0"` and you push new commits **without changing that string**, existing users get nothing ŌĆö Claude Code sees the same version and keeps the cached copy. The fix is to bump on every release, or omit `version` entirely.

Also (same Warning): **don't set `version` in both** `plugin.json` and the marketplace entry. The `plugin.json` value wins *silently*, so a stale manifest can mask the version you set in `marketplace.json`. Pick one home for the version number.

### Release channels

Channels fall out of this: point two marketplaces (`stable-tools`, `latest-tools`) at different `ref`s of the same repo and assign each to a user group via managed settings. The docs' caveat (verbatim spirit): **each channel must resolve to a *different* version** ŌĆö if two refs resolve to the same `version` string, Claude Code treats them as identical and skips the update. With omitted `version`, the distinct commit SHAs already distinguish the channels.

stage-4.heading: The trust problem: one click, a lot of code
stage-4.body:
Here is the part the convenience hides. Installing a plugin activates its skills, hooks, and MCP servers as **code running under one trust decision**, with your user privileges. The docs say it plainly: *"Plugins and marketplaces are highly trusted components that can execute arbitrary code on your machine with your user privileges. Only install plugins and add marketplaces from sources you trust."* The install UI repeats it: *"Anthropic does not control what MCP servers, files, or other software are included in plugins and cannot verify that they work as intended."*

Security researchers call this the **bundled trust radius**: *"A single plugin installation activates skills, MCP servers, hooks, and arbitrary binaries under a single, opaque click of consent"* (Pluto Security). The `plugin.json` manifest carries no signed authorship and no capability statement ŌĆö approving the apex of the plugin endorses every layer beneath it without enumeration.

This is not theoretical. Snyk's *ToxicSkills* audit of **3,984** third-party skills found **13.4% (534) had a critical security issue**, **76 contained confirmed malicious payloads**, **10.9% exposed secrets**, and **17.7% fetched untrusted third-party content** (Pluto Security, citing Snyk). And the runtime matters: per Pluto, *"The same `SKILL.md` is safely sandboxed on the Claude API but inherits full local host privileges when executed via Claude Code"* ŌĆö full filesystem and network. *"The threat is not in the file; it is in where it runs."*

### A demonstrated plugin-injection chain

PromptArmor demonstrated a full hijack via an injected marketplace plugin (source: promptarmor.com/resources/hijacking-claude-code-via-injected-marketplace-plugins):

1. **Install** ŌĆö a user adds a malicious marketplace from a public registry, often impersonating Anthropic branding.
2. **Permission bypass** ŌĆö a malicious **hook** fires and either overwrites `settings.local.json` to permanently allow dangerous commands like `curl`, or auto-approves command types *before the human-in-the-loop dialog appears*.
3. **Manipulation** ŌĆö the user runs an innocent-looking command (e.g. "Docs") that carries hidden **prompt-injection** instructions.
4. **Exfiltration** ŌĆö Claude, believing it needs a "RAG service," `curl`s the codebase context to the attacker's server ŌĆö now automatic, because the guardrails were already disabled in step 2.

Hooks are the primary weapon precisely because they run shell code on events, before tool-use prompts. A plugin is not a passive config bundle; it's an executable supply-chain dependency. (Note: Anthropic's own `claude-community` marketplace screens submissions with automated safety checks and pins each plugin to a reviewed commit SHA ŌĆö but that protection does **not** extend to arbitrary third-party marketplaces you add yourself.)

stage-4.heading: Governing the shelf: review, pin, restrict
When to use what
stage-4.body:
Not everything should be a plugin, and not every plugin needs a marketplace. Match the mechanism to the sharing need.

| Situation | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Personal workflow, one machine | standalone `.claude/skills/` | Short names (`/review`), zero packaging, instant iteration. |
| One project's quirk, not shared | project `.claude/` (committed) | Travels with the repo; no namespace ceremony. |
| Reusable across *your* projects | plugin via `--plugin-dir`, or `claude plugin init` in `~/.claude/skills/` | Versioning + one source of truth, no marketplace overhead yet. |
| Shared with the team, kept in sync | **plugin in a private marketplace** | The shelf: add once, install, pull updates by pushing to the repo. |
| Distributed to the community | `claude-community` marketplace (submitted + screened) | Discovery + automated safety screening + SHA-pinned catalog. |

The official docs' rule of thumb (verbatim Tip): *"Start with standalone configuration in `.claude/` for quick iteration, then convert to a plugin when you're ready to share."* Don't reach for a marketplace on day one of a skill ŌĆö reach for it when "three people rebuilt the same thing" becomes true.

### Common pitfalls / gotchas (consolidated)

- **Wrong directory for components.** `commands/`, `skills/`, `agents/`, `hooks/` go at the **plugin root**, never inside `.claude-plugin/`. Only `plugin.json` lives in `.claude-plugin/`.
- **Version string never bumped** ŌåÆ `/plugin update` is a silent no-op. Bump `version` every release, or omit it to use the commit SHA. Never set it in both `plugin.json` and the marketplace entry.
- **`../` and absolute paths break after install.** Plugins are copied to `~/.claude/plugins/cache`; reference internal files with `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`, persist update-surviving state under `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}`, and use symlinks if you must share files across plugins.
- **Relative-path sources fail on URL-added marketplaces.** A bare URL to `marketplace.json` downloads only that file, not the plugin dirs. Use git-hosted marketplaces (or `github`/`npm`/`url` plugin sources) when you need `./` paths.
- **`marketplace remove` uninstalls its plugins.** To refresh without losing installs, use `marketplace update`.
- **Reserved / impersonating marketplace names are rejected.** Don't name yours `anthropic-*` or anything that looks official.
- **Plugin skills not appearing?** Try `/reload-plugins`; check the `/plugin` Errors tab; as a last resort clear the cache: `rm -rf ~/.claude/plugins/cache`, restart, reinstall.
- **Trust is cumulative and opaque.** One install endorses every skill, hook, and MCP server in the bundle, transitively. Review before Trust; pin SHAs; keep the team marketplace private and `strictKnownMarketplaces`-allowlisted.

stage-5.heading: Why a CLAUDE.md note is not a control
stage-5.body:
Stage 4 gave the team a shared `CLAUDE.md`, skills, and a plugin or two. That file shapes what Claude *tries* to do. It does not change what Claude Code *allows*. The distinction is load-bearing, and the docs are blunt about it:

> Permission rules are enforced by Claude Code, not by the model. Instructions in your prompt or `CLAUDE.md` shape what Claude tries to do, but they don't change what Claude Code allows. ([permissions docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions))

This means every must-not you wrote in prose ŌĆö "never read `.env`", "don't `git push` to main", "don't install plugins from random marketplaces" ŌĆö is a *suggestion to a probabilistic system under adversarial pressure* (a prompt injection in a fetched web page, a malicious skill, a confused autocomplete). The model can be talked out of a CLAUDE.md rule. It cannot be talked out of a deny rule, because the deny rule lives in the harness and is evaluated before the tool call runs.

Governance is the act of moving the must-nots out of prose and into three enforcement layers:

- **Permission rules** ŌĆö allow/ask/deny glob patterns the harness checks on every tool call.
- **Permission modes** ŌĆö the autonomy dial that sets the baseline for how often it even asks.
- **Managed settings** ŌĆö org-level policy that sits *above* everything a developer can change, so a lower scope cannot relax it.

The second half of this stage is the money side of governance: **seats and plan gating** ŌĆö making sure you are not budgeting for access you already have, or assuming a feature is in your plan when it is a research preview. Both halves answer the same question: what does the *system* guarantee, independent of any individual's good intentions?

stage-5.heading: Settings precedence: the resolution order that makes governance possible
stage-5.body:
Claude Code reads configuration from five scopes and resolves them in a fixed order. Highest wins:

| Rank | Scope | File | Who controls it |
|------|-------|------|-----------------|
| 1 (highest) | **Managed** | `/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json` (Linux/WSL), `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/managed-settings.json` (macOS), `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` (Windows) | Org admin / MDM |
| 2 | **Command-line args** | `--permission-mode`, `--allowedTools`, `--disallowedTools`, etc. | Whoever launches the session |
| 3 | **Local project** | `.claude/settings.local.json` (gitignored) | Individual dev, this machine |
| 4 | **Shared project** | `.claude/settings.json` (committed) | The team, via the repo |
| 5 (lowest) | **User** | `~/.claude/settings.json` | Individual dev, all projects |

Source: [settings docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings). The legacy Windows path `C:\ProgramData\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` is **no longer supported as of v2.1.75** ŌĆö if you wrote MDM tooling against it, it silently stops applying. Migrate deployments to `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json`.

### Two different merge behaviors ŌĆö do not conflate them

For *most* scalar settings (`model`, `outputStyle`, `defaultMode`), higher precedence **overrides** lower: managed `model` beats user `model`, full stop.

But **permission rules do not override ŌĆö they merge (concatenate)** across all five scopes. This is the single most important mechanical fact in this stage and the reason governance is enforceable. It gets its own section below.

### The governance lever

Because managed settings sit at rank 1 and *cannot be overridden by a CLI flag* (rank 2), a managed deny rule is a hard floor. A developer cannot pass `--allowedTools 'Read(./.env.*)'` to climb over a managed `deny`. The docs are explicit: "a managed settings deny cannot be overridden by `--allowedTools`, and `--disallowedTools` can add restrictions beyond what managed settings define." That is what makes managed settings a *control* and not a *default*.

stage-5.heading: Permission rules: the access-control primitive
stage-5.body:
A permission rule is a tool-name glob pattern living in the `permissions` object of a settings file. Three arrays:

```json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(npm run *)",
      "Bash(git commit *)",
      "Read(~/.zshrc)"
    ],
    "ask": [
      "Bash(git push *)"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",
      "Read(./.env.*)",
      "Read(./secrets/**)",
      "Bash(curl *)"
    ]
  }
}
```

Manage them live with `/permissions`, which lists every rule and the settings file it came from.

### Rule shape

A rule is `Tool` or `Tool(specifier)`. A bare tool name matches every use:

- `Bash` ŌĆö every shell command. `Bash(*)` is equivalent. As a **deny** rule, a bare name *removes the tool from Claude's context entirely* ŌĆö Claude never even sees it. A scoped rule like `Bash(rm *)` leaves Bash available and blocks only matching calls.
- `Bash(git push *)` ŌĆö commands starting with `git push`. The space before `*` enforces a word boundary: `Bash(ls *)` matches `ls -la` but **not** `lsof`; `Bash(ls*)` matches both. `Bash(ls:*)` is an equivalent trailing-wildcard form (the `:*` suffix is only recognized at the *end* of a pattern). A single `*` spans multiple arguments, so `Bash(git * main)` matches `git push origin main` and `git merge main`.
- `Read(./.env.*)` ŌĆö file reads follow [gitignore semantics](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions). A bare filename matches at any depth: `Read(.env)` == `Read(**/.env)`. Anchors matter: `/path` is **relative to project root**, `//path` is a true absolute path, `~/path` is home, and a bare `path` or `./path` is **relative to the current directory**. `Read(/Users/alice/x)` is NOT absolute ŌĆö it is `<project>/Users/alice/x`; use `Read(//Users/alice/x)` for the real absolute path.
- `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` ŌĆö matches the hostname (case-insensitive). `WebFetch(domain:*.example.com)` matches subdomains at any depth but **not** the apex `example.com`.
- `mcp__github` / `mcp__github__*` / `mcp__github__get_*` ŌĆö MCP tools. The allow-side server segment must be glob-free. `mcp__*` (a tool-name glob) in a **deny** array kills every MCP tool across every server; as an *allow* glob, `mcp__*` is skipped with a warning and auto-approves nothing.

### Compound commands and process wrappers

Claude Code is shell-aware: a rule like `Bash(safe-cmd *)` does **not** authorize `safe-cmd && other-cmd` ŌĆö each subcommand (split on `&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`, `&`, newlines) must match independently. A fixed set of wrappers is stripped before matching, so `Bash(npm test *)` also matches `timeout 30 npm test` (recognized wrappers: `timeout`, `time`, `nice`, `nohup`, `stdbuf`, and bare `xargs`). Environment-runner wrappers like `npx`, `docker exec`, `devbox run`, and `mise exec` are **not** stripped ŌĆö `Bash(devbox run *)` would match `devbox run rm -rf .`, so write an explicit inner rule like `Bash(devbox run npm test)`.

### Match by input parameter (deny/ask only)

A newer form, `Tool(param:value)`, matches a top-level scalar input parameter ŌĆö useful for autonomy gating: `Agent(model:opus)`, `Agent(isolation:worktree)`, `Bash(run_in_background:true)`. This is **deny/ask only** ŌĆö an allow rule for one parameter wouldn't establish the whole call is safe. The value supports `*` as a wildcard; a parameter the model omits is never matched (so `Agent(model:*)` does not match a call that leaves `model` unset). Canonicalized fields (`command` for Bash, `file_path` for Read/Edit/Write, `url` for WebFetch, `path` for Grep/Glob) are *not* matchable this way ŌĆö `Bash(command:rm *)` is ignored with a startup warning; use `Bash(rm *)`.

All of the above is verified against the [permissions docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions).

stage-5.heading: How rules evaluate: deny ŌåÆ ask ŌåÆ allow, first-match-wins, arrays merge
stage-5.body:
This is the algorithm. Memorize it; it is the whole enforcement model.

### Evaluation order
