Rules are checked in the order **deny, then ask, then allow**. The first match in that order wins, and **specificity does not change the order**:

> A broad deny rule like `Bash(aws *)` blocks every matching call, including calls that also match a narrower allow rule like `Bash(aws s3 ls)`, so a deny rule cannot carry allowlist exceptions. ([permissions docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions))

So you cannot write `deny: Bash(aws *)` + `allow: Bash(aws s3 ls)` and expect the narrow allow to punch through. Deny is absolute for anything it matches. The same precedence holds between ask and allow: a matching `ask` rule prompts even when a more specific `allow` also matches. If you need exceptions, you invert: allow the safe specifics, and use a `PreToolUse` hook or a narrower deny for the dangerous ones.

### Arrays merge across scopes ŌĆö the floor property

The `allow`/`ask`/`deny` arrays from **all five scopes are concatenated** before evaluation. Combined with deny-first, this gives the property that makes org governance real:

> If a tool is denied at any level, no other level can allow it. ([permissions docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions))

Work the direction both ways:

- A **managed** `deny: Read(./.env.*)` is a floor. No user, project, local, or CLI allow can lift it. This is the mechanism behind "deny secret reads" org-wide.
- A **user** `deny` also floors a **project** `allow`. If a dev personally denies `Bash(git push *)` in `~/.claude/settings.json`, a repo's committed `allow: Bash(git push *)` cannot override it ŌĆö deny from any scope is evaluated before any allow.

### A worked trace

Managed: `deny: ["Read(./.env.*)"]`. Project: `allow: ["Read(./**)"]`. Claude tries to read `./.env.production`:

1. Merged deny array contains `Read(./.env.*)` ŌåÆ matches `./.env.production` ŌåÆ **denied**. Allow is never consulted. The project's broad allow is irrelevant.

This is why you put the secret-read denials and the destructive-command denials in **managed** settings, not the repo: the repo is editable by anyone with commit access; managed settings are not.

stage-5.heading: Permission modes: the orthogonal autonomy dial
stage-5.body:
Permission *rules* answer *what is allowed*. Permission *modes* answer *how aggressively Claude acts on what's allowed* ŌĆö how often it pauses to ask at all. They are orthogonal: a mode sets the baseline, rules layer on top, and **deny rules and explicit `ask` rules apply in every mode, including `bypassPermissions`** (allow rules have no effect in `bypassPermissions` because everything is already approved).

| Mode | Runs without asking | Best for | Set via |
|------|---------------------|----------|---------|
| `default` | Reads only | Sensitive work, getting started | baseline |
| `plan` | Reads + read-only shell (no edits at all; proposes a plan) | Exploring before changing | `Shift+Tab`, `/plan`, `--permission-mode plan` |
| `acceptEdits` | Reads + file edits + common fs commands (`mkdir`, `touch`, `rm`, `rmdir`, `mv`, `cp`, `sed`) in-scope | Iterating on code you'll review via `git diff` | `Shift+Tab`, flag, `defaultMode` |
| `auto` | Everything, with a classifier model checking each call | Long tasks, reducing prompt fatigue | `--permission-mode auto`, admin-gated |
| `dontAsk` | Only pre-approved (allow rules + read-only Bash); everything else auto-**denied** | Locked-down CI | `--permission-mode dontAsk` |
| `bypassPermissions` | Everything, no prompts | Isolated containers/VMs only | `--permission-mode bypassPermissions` / `--dangerously-skip-permissions` |

Source: [permission-modes docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes).

### Setting and cycling

```json
{ "permissions": { "defaultMode": "plan" } }
```

In-session, `Shift+Tab` cycles `default ŌåÆ acceptEdits ŌåÆ plan`. The other modes are deliberately *not* in the default cycle: `auto` slots in only when your account qualifies and you opt in; `bypassPermissions` appears only after you started with an enabling flag (`--permission-mode bypassPermissions`, `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, or `--allow-dangerously-skip-permissions`); `dontAsk` never appears in the cycle at all and must be set with `--permission-mode dontAsk`.

### Protected paths ŌĆö never auto-approved except in bypass

Writes to a fixed set of protected paths are **never auto-approved in any mode except `bypassPermissions`** ŌĆö and crucially, a `permissions.allow` rule does **not** pre-approve them (the safety check runs *before* allow rules). The set is broader than just `.git` and `.claude`: it includes `.config/git`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, `.husky`, `.cargo`, `.devcontainer`, `.yarn`, `.mvn`, plus files like `.gitconfig`, `.zshrc`, `.npmrc`, `.envrc`, `.mcp.json`, and `.claude.json`. In `auto` mode these route to the classifier; in `dontAsk` they are denied; in prompting modes you get a prompt.

### The two notes that bite teams

- **`defaultMode: "auto"` is ignored from project/local settings.** As of v2.1.142, Claude Code ignores `auto` set in `.claude/settings.json` or `.claude/settings.local.json` so a repository cannot grant itself auto mode. It must live in `~/.claude/settings.json` or managed settings.
- **`bypassPermissions` got more dangerous in v2.1.126.** It now executes writes to protected paths like `.git/` and `.claude/` that earlier versions still prompted for. Only `rm -rf /`, `rm -rf ~`, and explicit `ask` rules still stop it.

stage-5.heading: auto mode: a classifier, not a free pass
stage-5.body:
`auto` mode is worth its own section because teams reach for it to "reduce prompts" and misjudge what it guarantees. It is a **research preview** and requires **Claude Code v2.1.83 or later** ([permission-modes docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes)).

### What it actually does

Instead of prompting you, a **separate classifier model** reviews each non-trivial action before it runs and blocks anything that "escalates beyond your request, targets unrecognized infrastructure, or appears driven by hostile content Claude read." Reads and working-directory edits (outside protected paths) skip the classifier; shell commands and network calls go through it ŌĆö adding a round-trip and counting toward your token usage. The classifier sees user messages, tool calls, and your `CLAUDE.md`, but **tool results are stripped**, so hostile content in a fetched page can't manipulate it directly.

### Blocked by default (a sample of the real list)

- Downloading and executing code, like `curl | bash`; sending sensitive data to external endpoints
- Production deploys and migrations; mass cloud-storage deletion; granting IAM or repo permissions
- **Force push, or pushing directly to `main`**
- `git reset --hard`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git clean -fd`, `git stash drop`/`clear`, and `git commit --amend` on a HEAD not created this session (presumed to discard work)
- `terraform`/`pulumi`/`cdk`/`terragrunt destroy`, and applying a plan that destroys resources

Allowed by default: local file ops in the working dir, installing declared dependencies, reading `.env` and sending credentials to their matching API, read-only HTTP, and pushing to the branch you started on or one Claude created. Run `claude auto-mode defaults` to print the full lists.

### Three things to know before you trust it

1. **It drops your dangerous broad allow rules on entry.** Blanket `Bash(*)`/`PowerShell(*)`, wildcarded interpreters like `Bash(python*)`, package-manager run commands, and `Agent` allow rules are dropped while in auto mode (restored on exit). Narrow rules like `Bash(npm test)` carry over. So a repo that allow-listed `Bash(*)` does not get a blank check.
2. **Conversational boundaries are honored but fragile.** Telling Claude "don't deploy until I review" makes the classifier block matching actions ŌĆö but boundaries are not stored as rules; the classifier re-reads them from the transcript each check, so **context compaction can lose the message that stated it**. For a hard guarantee, use a deny rule, not a sentence.
3. **Admin-gated and model-gated.** On Team/Enterprise an admin must enable it in [Claude Code admin settings](https://claude.ai/admin-settings/claude-code), and can lock it off with `permissions.disableAutoMode: "disable"`. Model gating: Opus 4.6+ or Sonnet 4.6 on the Anthropic API; on Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry only **Opus 4.7 and Opus 4.8**, and off until `CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_AUTO_MODE=1` is set (v2.1.158+).

stage-5.heading: A team baseline: the three files you actually deploy
stage-5.body:
Here is a concrete, copyable governance baseline. Three files, three scopes, each doing the job its scope is good at.

### 1. Managed settings (admin-deployed, un-overridable floor)

`/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json` (Linux/WSL; adjust path per OS):

```json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": [
      "Read(./.env)",
      "Read(./.env.*)",
      "Read(./secrets/**)",
      "Read(~/.ssh/**)",
      "Read(~/.aws/**)",
      "Bash(curl *)",
      "Bash(wget *)"
    ],
    "disableBypassPermissionsMode": "disable",
    "disableAutoMode": "disable"
  },
  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
    { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins" }
  ],
  "allowManagedMcpServersOnly": true,
  "requiredMinimumVersion": "2.1.150"
}
```

Everything here is a floor: secret reads are denied org-wide and no dev can lift them; bypass/auto modes are off; only the approved marketplace can be added; only managed MCP servers run; and `requiredMinimumVersion` makes clients below 2.1.150 **refuse to start** (so a known-bad release can't be used). Note `requiredMinimumVersion` is the *hard* floor ŌĆö the related, non-managed `minimumVersion` only blocks auto-update/`claude update` from going below it and never blocks startup.

### 2. Shared project settings (committed, team conventions)

`.claude/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(npm run *)",
      "Bash(git commit *)",
      "Bash(git status)",
      "Bash(git diff *)"
    ],
    "ask": [
      "Bash(git push *)"
    ],
    "defaultMode": "plan"
  }
}
```

The repo grants the routine commands and forces a confirmation prompt on `git push`. `defaultMode: "plan"` means a fresh session starts read-only and proposes before it edits.

### 3. Local settings (gitignored, per-dev convenience)

`.claude/settings.local.json` ŌĆö a dev's personal allow-list additions for their own machine, e.g. `"allow": ["Bash(docker compose up *)"]`. It can loosen *only within* what managed/project allow; it can never override a higher-scope deny, because deny from any scope is evaluated first.

stage-5.heading: Managed-only keys: the org control surface
stage-5.body:
A specific set of keys are read **only** from managed settings; placing them in user or project files does nothing. These are the levers an admin actually pulls. Verified against the [permissions](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions) and [settings](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings) docs.

| Key | Effect |
|-----|--------|
| `strictKnownMarketplaces` | `[]` = no marketplaces addable at all; a list = only those exact sources (with `hostPattern`/`pathPattern` regex forms). Checked *before* any download. |
| `blockedMarketplaces` | Blocklist of marketplace sources; blocked sources are checked before download and never touch the filesystem. |
| `allowedMcpServers` / `deniedMcpServers` | Allow/deny specific MCP servers by name (`deniedMcpServers` still merges from all sources). |
| `allowManagedMcpServersOnly` | Only managed `allowedMcpServers` are respected (deny still merges from all sources). |
| `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly` | User/project **cannot define any** allow/ask/deny rules ŌĆö only managed rules apply. (Does not affect the MCP allowlist.) |
| `allowManagedHooksOnly` | Only managed/SDK/force-enabled-plugin hooks load; all user/project hooks blocked. |
| `strictPluginOnlyCustomization` | `true` blocks skills, agents, hooks, and MCP from user/project sources ŌĆö they must come from plugins or managed settings. Or an array like `["skills","hooks"]` to lock only those surfaces. |
| `requiredMinimumVersion` / `requiredMaximumVersion` | Refuse to start outside a version window (hard floor/ceiling). |
| `wslInheritsWindowsSettings` | WSL reads the Windows policy chain in addition to `/etc/claude-code`. |
| `forceLoginMethod` / `forceLoginOrgUUID` | Pin auth to a method / org. |
| `forceRemoteSettingsRefresh` | Fail-closed: block startup until remote managed settings are freshly fetched, exit if the fetch fails. |
| `blockedMarketplaces`, `pluginTrustMessage`, `allowAllClaudeAiMcps`, `channelsEnabled`, `allowedChannelPlugins` | Additional managed-only plugin/MCP/channel controls. |

Note the version-key trap: `requiredMinimumVersion` (managed-only, **blocks startup**) is distinct from `minimumVersion` (any scope, never blocks startup ŌĆö only gates auto-update). Use the former for enforcement.

### Drop-in directory

Managed settings can be split into `managed-settings.d/*.json`, merged **alphabetically** (`10-telemetry.json` ŌåÆ `20-security.json`): scalars override, **arrays concatenate and de-dupe**, objects deep-merge, dotfiles ignored. This lets MDM tooling ship security policy and telemetry policy as separate files without coordinating edits to one file.

### The escalating lockdown ladder

`strictPluginOnlyCustomization` and the `allowManaged*Only` family form a ratchet. Default: anyone can add skills/hooks/MCP locally. One notch: only plugins or managed settings can. Full: `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly` + `allowManagedHooksOnly` + `allowManagedMcpServersOnly` means the *entire* extension surface is admin-defined and a developer's machine cannot introduce a single rule, hook, or server you didn't ship.

stage-5.heading: Plugin governance: why this is the highest-leverage deny
stage-5.body:
Stage 4 introduced plugins as a convenience. This stage names the risk: **installing a plugin is one trust decision that activates its skills, hooks, and MCP servers as code**. A hook is an arbitrary shell command that runs on tool events. A skill is instructions the model will follow. An MCP server is a process with tools. One `/plugin install` turns all of that on at once.

This is not theoretical. An audit of open third-party marketplaces found **76 confirmed malicious payloads among ~3,984 skills (13.4% rated critical)**, and plugin-injection attacks against Claude Code have been demonstrated ([Pluto Security](https://pluto.security/blog/claude-extension-ecosystem-security-practitioner-guide/), [PromptArmor](https://www.promptarmor.com/resources/hijacking-claude-code-via-injected-marketplace-plugins)).

### The marketplace format you are gating

A marketplace is a `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` catalog at the repo root:

```json
{
  "name": "my-plugins",
  "owner": { "name": "Your Name" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "quality-review-plugin",
      "source": "./plugins/quality-review-plugin",
      "description": "Adds a quality-review skill for quick code reviews"
    }
  ]
}
```

Users add and install with:

```shell
/plugin marketplace add ./my-marketplace      # or owner/repo for a GitHub-hosted catalog
/plugin install quality-review-plugin@my-plugins
```

They refresh a local copy with `/plugin marketplace update`.

### The governance move

Don't tell people "only install from the approved marketplace." Enforce it. In managed settings:

```json
{
  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
    { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins" },
    { "source": "hostPattern", "hostPattern": "^github\\.acme\\.com$" }
  ]
}
```

`strictKnownMarketplaces: []` is complete lockdown ŌĆö no marketplace addable. A list restricts to exact matches, with `hostPattern`/`pathPattern` regex forms for self-hosted git (use `pathPattern: ".*"` to allow any filesystem path while still gating network sources). The check runs **before any network/filesystem operation**, and **on marketplace add and on plugin install, update, refresh, and auto-update** ŌĆö so a marketplace added before the policy existed gets frozen out at update time too. Exact matching does not normalize URLs (a trailing slash, `.git` suffix, or `ssh://` vs `https://` are distinct), so prefer a `hostPattern` over a literal URL. Pair it with `extraKnownMarketplaces` (a non-managed key) to *pre-register* the approved marketplace so devs don't even run `/plugin marketplace add`. Source: [plugin-marketplaces docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces).

stage-5.heading: Defense in depth: rules, hooks, and the sandbox
stage-5.body:
Permission rules are necessary but not sufficient, and the docs are careful about their boundaries. Three failure modes worth internalizing.

### Read/Edit deny rules don't stop subprocesses

> Read and Edit deny rules apply to Claude's built-in file tools and to file commands Claude Code recognizes in Bash, such as `cat`, `head`, `tail`, and `sed`. They do not apply to arbitrary subprocesses that read or write files indirectly, like a Python or Node script that opens files itself. ([permissions docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions))

So `deny: Read(./.env.*)` stops `cat .env` and the Read tool, but a `python -c 'print(open(".env").read())'` slips past the rule layer. For OS-level enforcement that blocks *all* processes, you need [sandboxing](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing) ŌĆö filesystem/network isolation for Bash and its children. Rules + sandbox are explicitly designed as complementary layers; the sandbox's filesystem boundary *merges* Read/Edit deny rules with the `sandbox.filesystem` settings, and its network boundary merges `WebFetch(domain:...)` rules with the sandbox `allowedDomains`/`deniedDomains`.

### Bash argument constraints are fragile

`Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)` does not constrain curl to GitHub ŌĆö `-X GET` before the URL, `https://`, a `-L` redirect, or `URL=... && curl $URL` all evade it. The doc's own recommendation: deny `curl`/`wget` wholesale and route allowed fetches through `WebFetch(domain:...)`, or validate in a `PreToolUse` hook. (Note: WebFetch alone does not block network access ŌĆö if Bash is allowed, Claude can still reach any URL via `curl`.)

### Hooks as runtime permission logic

A `PreToolUse` hook runs before the permission prompt and can deny, force a prompt, or allow. But the precedence is one-directional: a hook **cannot bypass a deny or ask rule** (those still apply regardless of what the hook returns, preserving the managed-deny floor), while a hook that exits **code 2 does** override an allow rule ŌĆö it stops the call before permission rules are even evaluated. The idiom "allow all Bash, block a few" is: put `Bash` in allow, then register a hook that rejects the specific dangerous commands. The hook is where you put logic too contextual for a glob.

stage-5.heading: Seats & plan gating: stop paying for access you already have
stage-5.body:
The other half of governance is not paying ŌĆö in money or in wrong assumptions ŌĆö for what your plan does or doesn't include. The single most common procurement mistake here is treating the Premium seat as the access gate for Claude Code. It is not.

### The fact that resets the budget

> Claude Code is included with **every** Team plan seat ŌĆö Standard *and* Premium. Premium only buys more usage for heavier workloads; it is **not** the access gate. ([support 11845131](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11845131), [support 9266767](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/9266767))

A Premium/Code-seat *requirement* applies only to **legacy Enterprise**; on new or self-serve Enterprise, Claude Code is included with every seat too. So if you are on a Team plan, you do not need to buy Premium seats to give engineers Claude Code; you buy Premium only for the people who will hit usage ceilings. Sizing the rollout as "N engineers ├Ś Premium" when most of them are light users is a straightforward overspend.

### Decision frame

| Question | Answer |
|----------|--------|
| Does every Team seat get Claude Code? | Yes ŌĆö Standard and Premium both. |
| What does Premium buy? | More usage, not access. |
| When is a Code/Premium seat *required*? | Legacy Enterprise only (not new/self-serve Enterprise). |
| Who toggles auto mode, Remote Control, web sessions org-wide? | An admin, in [Claude Code admin settings](https://claude.ai/admin-settings/claude-code). |

### The governance read

Plan gating *is* a control surface, just a commercial one. Admin-level toggles (auto mode on/off, Remote Control, web sessions, channels) live in the admin console and behave like managed settings: org-wide, not per-dev. Pair the seat decision with the managed-settings decision ŌĆö they're the two halves of "what is this org allowed to do."

stage-5.heading: Plan-gating the autonomous extras: don't budget a research preview as a feature
stage-5.body:
The flip side of "don't overpay for access you have" is "don't assume access you don't have." Two headline-grabbing capabilities are easy to write into a team plan that doesn't actually include them.

### Computer use and phone Dispatch

**Computer use** (Claude driving a desktop, enabled in the desktop-app settings) and **phone Dispatch** (Claude acting via a phone) are a **Pro/Max research preview on macOS and Windows** ([Anthropic blog](https://claude.com/blog/dispatch-and-computer-use), [support 12138966](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/12138966)).

The careful claim ŌĆö and the reason to state it carefully ŌĆö is this: the docs do **not** document Team/Enterprise *exclusion*. "Not on Team" is an *inference*, not a documented fact. So the accurate way to write it in a plan is:

> Computer use and phone Dispatch are **not part of the documented Team plan** ŌĆö they are a Pro/Max research preview. Do not budget a Team rollout around them.

The distinction matters because a research preview is unstable scope: it can change, move plans, or be withdrawn. Building a team workflow on top of it is building on sand, and assuming it's in your Team seat because you saw the announcement is exactly the recency-bias trap ŌĆö the announcement is salient, the plan-scoping footnote is not.

### The general rule

When a capability is described as a "research preview," treat it as **off the governance map**: don't deny-rule around it (it may not exist for your users), don't budget for it, and don't write runbooks that depend on it. Revisit at GA. The verified-claims discipline here ŌĆö separating *documented fact* from *reasonable inference* ŌĆö is itself the skill: governance decisions made on inferences should be labeled as such, so the next person knows what to recheck when the docs update.

stage-5.heading: When to use what: a governance decision table
stage-5.body:
Put the right control at the right scope. The recurring mistake is reaching for the model (CLAUDE.md) when the situation calls for the harness (a rule), or reaching for a rule when it calls for OS-level isolation (the sandbox).

| Goal | Wrong tool | Right tool | Scope |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Never read secrets | CLAUDE.md "don't read .env" | `deny: Read(./.env.*)` | **Managed** (floor) |
| Block secrets even from a `python` subprocess | deny rule alone | Sandbox filesystem isolation | Managed + sandbox |
| Confirm before pushing | CLAUDE.md note | `ask: Bash(git push *)` | Project |
| Only approved plugins | "please use our marketplace" | `strictKnownMarketplaces` | **Managed** |
| No untrusted MCP servers | trust | `allowManagedMcpServersOnly` | **Managed** |
| Read-only exploration by default | habit | `defaultMode: "plan"` | Project |
| Unattended CI run, locked down | `bypassPermissions` | `dontAsk` + explicit allow-list | CLI / project |
| Long autonomous task, some oversight | `bypassPermissions` | `auto` mode | User / CLI |
| Fully autonomous in a throwaway container | nothing | `bypassPermissions` | CLI, container only |
| Dev can't introduce *any* local rule | hope | `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly` | **Managed** |

### Common pitfalls / gotchas, consolidated

- **Permission rules MERGE; other settings OVERRIDE.** Forgetting this makes a managed deny look like it "isn't applying" when in fact it's working ŌĆö the lower-scope allow is simply being out-prioritized.
- **Deny carries no exceptions.** `deny: Bash(aws *)` + `allow: Bash(aws s3 ls)` blocks `aws s3 ls`. Invert to a narrow allow + hook instead.
- **`/path` is project-relative, not absolute.** Use `//path` for true absolute. The single most common Read-rule bug.
- **Allow rules don't pre-approve protected-path writes** (`.git`, `.claude`, `.vscode`, `.npmrc`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö the safety check runs before allow rules in every mode but `bypassPermissions`.
- **`defaultMode: "auto"` is ignored from repo settings** (v2.1.142+). Set it in `~/.claude` or managed.
- **`bypassPermissions` writes to `.git`/`.claude`** as of v2.1.126 ŌĆö containers/VMs only, and lock it off org-wide with `disableBypassPermissionsMode: "disable"`.
- **Bash argument filters are evadable** ŌĆö deny the binary, allow via `WebFetch(domain:...)` or a hook.
- **A plugin install is one trust decision for skills+hooks+MCP.** Gate the marketplace, don't gate intentions.
- **`requiredMinimumVersion` (managed, blocks startup) ŌēĀ `minimumVersion` (any scope, gates auto-update only).** Use the former to refuse known-bad releases.
- **Premium ŌēĀ access to Claude Code on Team.** Buy Premium for usage headroom, not to unlock the tool.
- **Computer use / Dispatch are a Pro/Max research preview**, not documented Team features ŌĆö don't plan around them.

stage-6.heading: The decision you're actually making
stage-6.body:
By now you know the parts: skills, subagents, hooks, MCP servers, permission rules, `AGENTS.md`. That was the *vocabulary*. This stage is the *grammar* ŌĆö the rule that tells you where each part goes so a five-person team (or fifty) shares one coherent setup instead of fifty divergent ones.

The question your team is waiting on is not "what is a skill?" It is: **when someone writes a useful skill, where does it live so everyone gets it, it stays versioned, and it works whether they drive Claude Code or Codex?** Get this wrong and you hit the two classic failure modes:

- **Sprawl** ŌĆö every engineer hand-copies skills into their own `~/.claude/`, they drift, and "works on my machine" infects your agent tooling.
- **Lock-in** ŌĆö everything lives in one person's local config, nobody can review it, and onboarding a new hire is a 40-minute Slack thread.

The fix is a single mental model: **the four-tier model**. Decide the tier for each shared thing *once*, write it down, and the rest is mechanics. The verified team-setup guidance ([Anthropic, *Steering Claude Code*](https://claude.com/blog/steering-claude-code-skills-hooks-rules-subagents-and-more); [*Settings*](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)) is exactly this: shared setup falls into four tiers by **where each thing lives**.

stage-6.heading: The four-tier model
stage-6.body:
Every shared artifact belongs to exactly one of four tiers, distinguished by **scope of ownership** and **distribution mechanism**:

| Tier | What lives here | Mechanism | Who owns it | Committed to git? |
|------|----------------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **1. Project config** | Rules, project-specific skills/agents, hooks, permission allowlist, marketplace declaration | `.claude/` dir + `CLAUDE.md`/`AGENTS.md` in the repo | The repo / its maintainers | Yes ŌĆö in the repo |
| **2. Cross-project tools** | Skills, agents, MCP servers, hooks reused across many repos | A **plugin marketplace** (a git repo of plugins) | A platform/devtools team | Yes ŌĆö in the marketplace repo |
| **3. Org policy** | Mandatory guardrails: which MCP servers are allowed, version floors, forced login org, marketplace allowlist | **Managed settings** (`managed-settings.json`, MDM) | IT / security | Deployed by IT, not editable by users |
| **4. Personal config** | Individual preferences: model choice, theme, personal experimental skills | `~/.claude/settings.json`, `~/.codex/config.toml` | Each engineer | No ŌĆö local only |

### The decision rule

Ask these questions in order; the first "yes" picks the tier:

1. **Is it a non-negotiable the org must enforce?** ŌåÆ Tier 3 (managed settings ŌĆö it sits at the top of precedence and can't be overridden).
2. **Is it reused across repos by the team?** ŌåÆ Tier 2 (marketplace plugin).
3. **Is it specific to one repo?** ŌåÆ Tier 1 (project config in `.claude/`).
4. Otherwise ŌåÆ Tier 4 (personal).

### Precedence at runtime

The tiers also map onto a **precedence order** when settings collide. Per [the settings docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings), Claude Code resolves settings highest-to-lowest:

**Managed ŌåÆ command-line args ŌåÆ Local ŌåÆ Project ŌåÆ User.**

Managed (Tier 3) always wins; personal *user-scope* defaults (Tier 4) lose to everything. Note one wrinkle: a repo's `.claude/settings.local.json` is the **Local** scope, which *outranks* the committed Project `.claude/settings.json` ŌĆö so a personal per-repo override beats the team's committed project setting, but still loses to managed. This precedence is deliberate: org policy can't be overridden by a clever engineer, and a personal experiment in one repo doesn't silently leak into the team's shared config.

One exception runs through everything below: **permission rules merge, they don't override** (covered in Tier 1).

stage-6.heading: Tier 1 ŌĆö Project config in the repo
stage-6.body:
This is the tier most teams already half-have. Everything that is *true about this repo specifically* lives in `.claude/` and is committed, so cloning the repo gives you a fully-configured agent.

### Layout

```text
my-service/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ CLAUDE.md                 # rules/context for Claude Code
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ AGENTS.md                 # same role for Codex & other agents (see portability)
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .claude/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ settings.json         # committed: team-shared settings (Project scope)
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ settings.local.json   # gitignored: personal per-repo overrides (Local scope)
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/
    Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ run-migrations/SKILL.md
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/
    Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ db-reviewer.md
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/                # hook scripts; the hook *declarations* live in settings.json
```

### Committed vs local

The single most important split inside Tier 1: **`.claude/settings.json` is committed and shared (Project scope); `.claude/settings.local.json` is gitignored and personal (Local scope)** ([Settings docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)). Put the team's permission allowlist, env vars, and hooks in `settings.json`. Put your personal one-off allows in `settings.local.json` ŌĆö and remember Local outranks Project, so your local file wins any conflict with the committed one.

```json
// .claude/settings.json ŌĆö committed, the whole team gets this
{
  "$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json",
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(npm run test:*)", "Bash(npm run lint)"],
    "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Read(./.env.*)", "Read(./secrets/**)"],
    "ask": ["Bash(git push:*)"]
  },
  "env": { "NODE_ENV": "development" }
}
```

### Permissions merge ŌĆö they don't override

The one setting that breaks the precedence-override rule: **permission rules (`allow`/`ask`/`deny`) merge across every scope rather than override** ([Settings docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)). A project `deny` and a managed `deny` both apply; arrays from all tiers combine. That's why a project can safely *add* `deny` rules on top of org policy without weakening it ŌĆö and why a project **cannot remove** an org-level `deny`. Layered, additive guardrails are the design intent.

stage-6.heading: Tier 2 ŌĆö Cross-project tools in a marketplace
stage-6.body:
This is the tier that turns "I wrote a nice skill" into "the team has it." When a skill, agent, or MCP integration is useful across repos, you do **not** copy it into each `.claude/`. You package it as a **plugin** and distribute it through a **plugin marketplace** ([Create plugins](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins); [Marketplaces](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces)).

### Why a marketplace instead of copying

| Use standalone `.claude/` (Tier 1) | Use a plugin/marketplace (Tier 2) |
|------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Single project | Reused across multiple projects |
| Personal / experimental | Shared with the team or community |
| Short skill names (`/deploy`) | Versioned, namespaced (`/my-plugin:deploy`) |
| Manual copy to share | `/plugin install`, central updates |

A marketplace gives you **centralized discovery, version tracking, automatic updates, and one source of truth.** Bump the version in one repo; every engineer's next `/plugin update` (or background auto-update) pulls it. No drift.

### Anatomy of a plugin

A plugin is a directory with a manifest in `.claude-plugin/` plus component dirs *at the plugin root* ŌĆö **not** inside `.claude-plugin/`:

```text
quality-tools/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ plugin.json          # ONLY the manifest goes here
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ code-review/SKILL.md
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ security-reviewer.md
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ hooks.json
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .mcp.json                # MCP servers the plugin ships
```

```json
// .claude-plugin/plugin.json
{
  "name": "quality-tools",
  "description": "Shared code-review skill + security agent",
  "version": "1.2.0",
  "author": { "name": "DevTools Team" }
}
```

The `name` becomes the namespace: this plugin's `code-review` skill is invoked `/quality-tools:code-review`. Namespacing is what keeps two teams' `deploy` skills from colliding. Inside hooks and MCP configs, reference files with `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` ŌĆö the plugin is copied to a cache on install, so relative-to-cwd paths won't resolve (more in Pitfalls).

stage-6.heading: Tier 2 mechanics ŌĆö the marketplace file
stage-6.body:
A **marketplace** is itself just a git repo containing a catalog at `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` that lists plugins and where to fetch each one ([Marketplaces](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces)). Required top-level fields are `name`, `owner` (with a required `owner.name`), and `plugins`.

```json
// .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
{
  "name": "acme-tools",
  "owner": { "name": "DevTools Team", "email": "devtools@acme.com" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "quality-tools",
      "source": "./plugins/quality-tools",
      "description": "Code review + security agent"
    },
    {
      "name": "deploy-tools",
      "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/deploy-plugin" }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Plugin source types (the load-bearing field)

Each plugin's `source` says where to fetch *that plugin*. (Distinct from the *marketplace* source ŌĆö where the catalog itself lives ŌĆö which supports `ref` but not `sha`.) The choices:

| `source` | Form | Use when |
|----------|------|----------|
| Relative path | `"./plugins/x"` (must start with `./`, resolved from the marketplace root) | Plugin lives in the same marketplace repo |
| `github` | `{ "source": "github", "repo": "org/repo", "ref": "v2", "sha": "<40-char>" }` | Plugin in a separate GitHub repo |
| `url` | `{ "source": "url", "url": "https://gitlab.com/...git", "ref": "...", "sha": "..." }` | Any git host (`.git` suffix optional) |
| `git-subdir` | `{ "source": "git-subdir", "url": "...", "path": "tools/plugin" }` | Plugin in a **monorepo** subdir (sparse clone) |
| `npm` | `{ "source": "npm", "package": "@acme/plugin", "version": "^2.0", "registry": "https://npm.example.com" }` | Distribute via npm (public or private registry) |

### Versioning ŌĆö the part teams get wrong

Claude Code resolves a plugin's version from the first of these that is set: (1) `version` in `plugin.json`, (2) `version` in the marketplace entry, (3) the git commit SHA of the source.

- **For internal, fast-iterating plugins, omit `version` entirely** on git-based sources (`github`/`url`/`git-subdir`/relative-in-git). Every commit then counts as a new version ŌĆö the simplest setup.
- **For stable releases, set `version` in `plugin.json` and bump it on every release.** If `plugin.json` says `"1.0.0"` and you push new commits *without* changing it, existing users get **nothing** ŌĆö Claude Code sees the same version and keeps the cache.
- **Never set `version` in both `plugin.json` and the marketplace entry** ŌĆö the `plugin.json` value wins *silently* and can mask the marketplace one.
- **Pin with `sha` for reproducibility.** When both `ref` and `sha` are set, the `sha` is the effective pin; on GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket the install survives even if the branch named by `ref` is later deleted (some hosts like AWS CodeCommit can't fetch by SHA, so there the `ref` must still exist).

### Installing

```shell
/plugin marketplace add acme-corp/claude-plugins   # GitHub owner/repo shorthand (append @ref to pin)
/plugin install quality-tools@acme-tools           # plugin@marketplace
/plugin marketplace update                          # pull new versions
```

Non-interactive equivalents exist for CI and scripting:

```bash
claude plugin marketplace add acme-corp/claude-plugins --scope project
claude plugin install quality-tools@acme-tools
claude plugin validate .   # run this in CI before publishing
```

`--scope project` writes the declaration into the committed `.claude/settings.json` (the Tier-1/Tier-2 bridge below), rather than the per-user default.

stage-6.heading: Tier 2 ŌĆö making the marketplace mandatory for the team
Tier 3 ŌĆö Org policy in managed settings
stage-6.body:
Tiers 1 and 2 are *opt-in conveniences*. Tier 3 is *enforcement*. Managed settings are deployed by IT (MDM, or a file dropped by config management) and **cannot be overridden** by user, project, or local config ŌĆö they sit at the top of the precedence order ([Settings](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)).

### Where the file lives

| OS | Managed settings path |
|----|----------------------|
| macOS | `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/managed-settings.json` |
| Linux / WSL | `/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json` |
| Windows | `C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` |

Note: the legacy Windows path `C:\ProgramData\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json` is **no longer supported** as of v2.1.75 ŌĆö use the `Program Files` path. File-based managed settings also merge with drop-ins under `managed-settings.d/*.json` (merged alphabetically after the base file; later files override scalars, arrays concatenate and de-dupe, objects deep-merge). This lets independent teams (telemetry, security) ship separate policy fragments ŌĆö e.g. `10-telemetry.json`, `20-security.json` ŌĆö without editing one shared file.

### What belongs here

Only true non-negotiables ŌĆö the things security/compliance must guarantee regardless of what any engineer does:

```json
// managed-settings.json ŌĆö deployed by IT, wins over everything
{
  "forceLoginOrgUUID": "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx",
  "requiredMinimumVersion": "2.1.150",
  "allowManagedMcpServersOnly": true,
  "allowedMcpServers": [{ "serverName": "github" }],
  "deniedMcpServers": [{ "serverName": "filesystem" }],
  "permissions": { "deny": ["Bash(curl *)", "Read(/etc/**)"] },
  "strictKnownMarketplaces": [
    { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/approved-plugins" }
  ]
}
```

Key semantics: `requiredMinimumVersion` blocks startup below the floor (but `claude update` still runs). `deniedMcpServers` takes precedence over `allowedMcpServers`. `forceLoginOrgUUID` accepts a single UUID or an array (an empty array fails closed). `strictKnownMarketplaces` is the Tier-3 control over Tier 2: an empty array `[]` is a full lockdown (no marketplaces can be added); a list is an allowlist. It only *restricts* ŌĆö it does not register anything, so pair it with `extraKnownMarketplaces` in the same managed file to *also* auto-register the approved one. Matching is **exact** and does not normalize URLs, so a trailing slash or `.git` suffix breaks the match ŌĆö use a `hostPattern` entry (regex on the host) instead of a literal URL when your git host is reachable by multiple URL forms.

### When to reach for Tier 3

Managed settings are heavyweight (IT deployment, no per-repo flexibility). Use them only for: forced auth/org binding, version floors, MCP allow/deny-listing, hard `deny` rules, and marketplace allowlisting. Everything else stays in Tier 1/2. Don't put convenience here "to be safe" ŌĆö you lose all per-repo flexibility and gain nothing.

stage-6.heading: Tier 4 ŌĆö Personal config, kept out of everyone's way
stage-6.body:
The last tier is the pressure-release valve. Engineers have preferences and experiments that **should not** leak into shared config. These live in user-scope files that aren't committed and lose to every higher tier.

```text
~/.claude/settings.json     # personal Claude Code defaults (model, theme, env)
~/.claude/skills/           # personal experimental skills
~/.codex/config.toml        # personal Codex config (see portability section)
```

The `.claude/settings.local.json` inside a repo is the *project-scoped* personal override ŌĆö same idea, but scoped to one repo and gitignored. It's the **Local** scope, so it outranks the committed Project `settings.json` but still loses to managed. Use it for "I want to allow `Bash(docker:*)` in *this* repo only" without committing it to the team.

### The anti-pattern this prevents

The whole reason Tier 4 exists as a named tier is to give people a *legitimate* place to put personal stuff so they stop polluting Tier 1. If an engineer's pet skill is genuinely useful, the move is to **promote it to Tier 2** (package as a plugin, PR to the marketplace) ŌĆö not to quietly commit it to a project's `.claude/skills/`. "Promote personal ŌåÆ marketplace" is the healthy lifecycle; "sneak personal into project config" is the rot.

stage-6.heading: The shared library ŌĆö the assembled output of the four tiers
stage-6.body:
Now the second concept. A **shared library** is not a fifth tier or a new file format ŌĆö it is the **assembled output** of the four tiers: *one* set of skills, agents, and tools the whole team uses, versioned and distributed, working across both Claude Code and Codex ([Steering Claude Code](https://claude.com/blog/steering-claude-code-skills-hooks-rules-subagents-and-more); [Skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)).

Concretely, the team's shared library = **a plugin marketplace (Tier 2) + repo `.claude/` and `AGENTS.md` (Tier 1) + portability so it runs on Codex too.**

### What's in it

| Component | Authored as | Distributed via |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Cross-project skills | `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Marketplace plugin |
| Cross-project agents | `agents/<name>.md` | Marketplace plugin |
| Shared MCP servers | `.mcp.json` in plugin / `config.toml` | Marketplace plugin / Codex config |
| Shared hooks | `hooks/hooks.json` | Marketplace plugin |
| Per-repo rules | `CLAUDE.md` + `AGENTS.md` | Committed in each repo |

The defining property is **single-sourcing**: the `code-review` skill exists in *one* place (the marketplace repo). Claude Code installs it via `/plugin install`. Codex picks it up because the same `SKILL.md` format is portable. Nobody maintains two copies.

### SKILL.md is the shared atom

The reason one library can span both tools is that the **`SKILL.md` format is nearly identical** across them: YAML frontmatter (`name` is implicit from the directory in Claude Code; `description` is required by both) plus a markdown body.

```markdown
---
name: code-review
description: Reviews a diff for bugs, security, and performance. Use when reviewing PRs or recent changes.
---

When reviewing code, check for: bugs/edge cases, security concerns,
performance issues, and test coverage. Be concise and actionable.
```

The `description` is load-bearing ŌĆö it's what both Claude and Codex use to decide *when* to auto-invoke the skill (progressive disclosure loads only names + descriptions first, then the body on demand). Write it as "what it does + when to use it," not just a label.

stage-6.heading: Cross-tool portability ŌĆö making the library work on Codex
stage-6.body:
"Works across both Claude and Codex" is a real engineering requirement, not a slogan. Here's the concrete mapping, verified against the [Codex skills](https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills), [config](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic), [MCP](https://developers.openai.com/codex/mcp), and [AGENTS.md](https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md) docs.

### The portability table

| Concept | Claude Code | Codex |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| Per-repo rules | `CLAUDE.md` | `AGENTS.md` (rootŌåÆcwd, concatenated; closest wins) |
| Project skills | `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | `.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` |
| User skills | `~/.claude/skills/` | `~/.agents/skills/` |
| Admin skills | (managed settings) | `/etc/codex/skills/` |
| MCP servers | `.mcp.json` / settings | `[mcp_servers.<name>]` in `config.toml` |
| Settings file | `settings.json` (JSON) | `config.toml` (TOML) |

### Two practical portability moves

**1. Author rules once, mirror to both filenames.** Codex reads `AGENTS.md` and walks the tree from repo root down to cwd, **concatenating** each file it finds (joined with blank lines); because the closest file appears last in the combined prompt, **the closest file wins** on conflicts, and an explicit chat prompt overrides everything. (Codex also checks `AGENTS.override.md` ahead of `AGENTS.md` in the same directory.) Claude reads `CLAUDE.md`. Keep one canonical source and generate or symlink the other so they never drift ŌĆö this repo's own convention is `CLAUDE.md` canonical, `AGENTS.md` the Codex-facing mirror.

**2. Codex skills live under `.agents/skills`, not `.codex/skills`.** Per the [Codex skills doc](https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills), Codex scans `.agents/skills` at the cwd, walking up through parent dirs to the repo root, plus `$HOME/.agents/skills` and `/etc/codex/skills`. The `SKILL.md` body is portable; only the directory and the per-tool config files differ.

### Codex MCP config

MCP servers are the most portable component ŌĆö same servers, different config syntax. For a stdio server:

```toml
# ~/.codex/config.toml  (or .codex/config.toml in a trusted project)
[mcp_servers.github]
command = "npx"
args = ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"]

[mcp_servers.github.env]
GITHUB_TOKEN = "..."
```

Or non-interactively: `codex mcp add github --env GITHUB_TOKEN=... -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-github`. (Codex also supports `env_vars = [...]` to forward named host variables instead of inlining secrets, and `url`-based HTTP servers in place of `command`/`args`.) Disable a skill without deleting it via `[[skills.config]]` with its `path` and `enabled = false`, then restart Codex:

```toml
[[skills.config]]
path = "/path/to/skill/SKILL.md"
enabled = false
```

stage-6.heading: Worked example ŌĆö a real team setup
stage-6.body:
Putting all four tiers together for a 6-person team that ships a Python service and a TS frontend, drives both Claude Code and Codex.

### The marketplace repo (Tier 2 ŌĆö the shared library's home)

```text
acme-corp/claude-plugins/          # one repo, the source of truth
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/marketplace.json
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ plugins/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ quality-tools/             # code-review skill + security agent + lint hook
    Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/plugin.json
    Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ skills/code-review/SKILL.md
    Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ agents/security-reviewer.md
    Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ hooks/hooks.json
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ data-tools/                # shared MCP servers (db, internal API)
        Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .claude-plugin/plugin.json
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .mcp.json
```

### Each service repo (Tier 1)

```text
acme-corp/payments-service/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ CLAUDE.md            # ŌåÉ canonical rules
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ AGENTS.md            # ŌåÉ mirror for Codex
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ .agents/skills/      # ŌåÉ Codex picks up project skills here
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ .claude/
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ settings.json    # declares the marketplace + enables plugins + repo permissions
```

```json
// payments-service/.claude/settings.json
{
  "extraKnownMarketplaces": {
    "acme-tools": { "source": { "source": "github", "repo": "acme-corp/claude-plugins" } }
  },
  "enabledPlugins": { "quality-tools@acme-tools": true, "data-tools@acme-tools": true },
  "permissions": { "allow": ["Bash(pytest:*)"], "deny": ["Read(./.env)"] }
}
```

### IT deployment (Tier 3)

`/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json` sets `requiredMinimumVersion`, `allowManagedMcpServersOnly: true`, and `strictKnownMarketplaces` allowlisting only `acme-corp/claude-plugins` ŌĆö paired with `extraKnownMarketplaces` so the approved marketplace also auto-registers.

### Each engineer (Tier 4)

`~/.claude/settings.json` picks their model and theme; `~/.codex/config.toml` carries their personal Codex prefs. Nothing team-relevant lives here.

**Result:** clone any repo ŌåÆ trust folder ŌåÆ prompted to install the exact shared library ŌåÆ identical skills/agents/MCP whether you open Claude Code or Codex. Bump `quality-tools` to `1.3.0`, everyone gets it on next `/plugin update`.

stage-6.heading: When to use what ŌĆö the decision table
stage-6.body:
The full lookup for "I have a thing ŌĆö which tier?"

| You have... | Tier | Where it goes |
|-------------|------|---------------|
| Rules about *this repo* | 1 | `CLAUDE.md` + `AGENTS.md` |
| A repo-only helper skill | 1 | `.claude/skills/` (+ `.agents/skills/` for Codex) |
| The team's permission allowlist for a repo | 1 | `.claude/settings.json` (committed, Project scope) |
| A personal allow for one repo | 4 | `.claude/settings.local.json` (gitignored, Local scope) |
| A skill/agent used in 2+ repos | 2 | Marketplace plugin |
| A shared MCP server (internal DB, API) | 2 | Plugin `.mcp.json` / Codex `config.toml` |
| "You may only add *our* marketplace" | 3 | `strictKnownMarketplaces` (managed) |
| "Everyone must be on Ōēź v2.1.150" | 3 | `requiredMinimumVersion` (managed) |
| "Block `curl`, force org login" | 3 | `permissions.deny` / `forceLoginOrgUUID` (managed) |
| Your model/theme preference | 4 | `~/.claude/settings.json` |
| An experimental skill you're iterating on | 4 ŌåÆ 2 | `~/.claude/skills/`, then promote to marketplace |

### Heuristics

- **Reuse count Ōēź 2 repos ŌåÆ promote from Tier 1 to Tier 2.** Copying is the smell that says "make it a plugin."
- **Tier 3 is for guarantees, not conveniences.** If you can't articulate it as a security/compliance must, it doesn't belong in managed settings.
- **Tier 4 is a staging area, not a graveyard.** Personal-useful things should graduate to Tier 2.

stage-6.heading: Common pitfalls and gotchas
stage-6.body:
The failure modes that bite teams, with the fix:

### Plugin structure
- **Putting `skills/`, `agents/`, `hooks/` *inside* `.claude-plugin/`.** Only `plugin.json` goes in `.claude-plugin/`; all component dirs sit at the plugin root. This is the #1 "my plugin doesn't load" cause.
- **Referencing `../shared-utils` from a plugin.** Plugins are copied to `~/.claude/plugins/cache`; outside files aren't copied (the validator flags `..` as path traversal). Use `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` for in-plugin paths, or symlinks for shared assets.

### Versioning
- **Setting `version` in both `plugin.json` and the marketplace entry.** The `plugin.json` value wins *silently*, masking the marketplace one. Pick one place.
- **Forgetting to bump `version`.** If `plugin.json` says `"1.0.0"` and you push new commits without changing it, existing users get **nothing** ŌĆö Claude Code sees the same version and keeps the cache. Either bump on every release, or omit `version` on a git-based source so each commit SHA counts as a new version.

### Settings & precedence
- **Expecting a project setting to override managed.** It can't ŌĆö managed (Tier 3) is the top of precedence.
- **Forgetting Local beats Project.** A stray `.claude/settings.local.json` silently overrides the committed team `settings.json` (it's the higher-precedence Local scope). When a teammate's behavior diverges, check their local file first.
- **Confusing override vs merge.** Most settings *override* by precedence; **permission rules merge** across tiers. A project can add `deny` rules on top of org policy but can't remove an org `deny`.
- **`strictKnownMarketplaces` exact-matching.** A trailing slash or `.git` suffix makes the URL not match (no normalization). Use `hostPattern` (regex on host) for robustness, and remember it only restricts ŌĆö pair with `extraKnownMarketplaces` to actually register the approved marketplace.

### Portability
- **Assuming Codex reads `.claude/skills`.** It reads `.agents/skills` (repo, walking up to root) and `~/.agents/skills`, plus `/etc/codex/skills`. The `SKILL.md` body is portable; the directory and config file (`config.toml` vs `settings.json`) are not. Mirror, don't assume.
- **Letting `CLAUDE.md` and `AGENTS.md` drift.** Make one canonical and generate/symlink the other, or the two tools will behave differently. Note Codex caps the concatenated instruction chain at `project_doc_max_bytes` (32 KiB default) ŌĆö keep root rules lean.

### Process
- **No CI validation.** Run `claude plugin validate .` in the marketplace repo's CI ŌĆö pointed at the marketplace dir it checks `marketplace.json` (schema, duplicate plugin names, source path traversal, version mismatches); pointed at a plugin dir (`claude plugin validate ./plugins/my-plugin`) it also checks each skill/agent/hook's frontmatter and `hooks.json` syntax. Run both before changes reach the team.

stage-6.heading: What you commit to ŌĆö the team charter
stage-6.body:
This stage is a *decision* stage, so end with the commitments your team writes down. A one-page charter that answers, concretely:

1. **Where is our shared library?** ŌåÆ one marketplace repo (e.g. `acme-corp/claude-plugins`). One source of truth.
2. **How does a repo opt in?** ŌåÆ `extraKnownMarketplaces` + `enabledPlugins` committed in `.claude/settings.json`, so cloning a repo provisions the agent.
3. **What's mandatory vs optional?** ŌåÆ mandatory guardrails in managed settings (Tier 3); everything else is opt-in plugins (Tier 2). If you lock down Tier 2, pair `strictKnownMarketplaces` with `extraKnownMarketplaces`.
4. **How do we stay portable?** ŌåÆ canonical `CLAUDE.md` mirrored to `AGENTS.md`; portable `SKILL.md`; project skills in both `.claude/skills` and `.agents/skills`; MCP servers expressed in both `.mcp.json` and `config.toml`.
5. **How does a personal skill become a team skill?** ŌåÆ PR it into the marketplace, bump the version. Tier 4 ŌåÆ Tier 2 is the only legitimate promotion path.
6. **How do we ship changes?** ŌåÆ bump `version` (or omit it and rely on commit SHA), push, `claude plugin validate .` in CI; engineers pick it up on `/plugin update` or background auto-update.

The four-tier model isn't bureaucracy ŌĆö it's the smallest set of decisions that makes a shared setup *reproducible, reviewable, and portable*. Decide each thing's tier once, write the charter, and the mechanics in this stage are all you need to execute it.

### Sources
- [Steering Claude Code: skills, hooks, rules, subagents](https://claude.com/blog/steering-claude-code-skills-hooks-rules-subagents-and-more)
- [Claude Code Settings](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings)
- [Create plugins](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins) ┬Ę [Plugin marketplaces](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces) ┬Ę [Skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)
- [Codex skills](https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills) ┬Ę [Codex config](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic) ┬Ę [Codex MCP](https://developers.openai.com/codex/mcp) ┬Ę [Codex AGENTS.md](https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md) ┬Ę [AGENTS.md spec](https://agents.md)
